topics.title: Overview of Stochastic Processes and Their Relevance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT867 - STOCHASTIC DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Stochastic Processes and Their Relevance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stochastic Process** is formally defined as a **family (or collection) of random variables** $\{X(t) : t \in T\}$ defined on a common probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$, where the index set $T$ typically represents **time**. For each fixed time index $t \in T$, the quantity $X(t)$ is a random variable; for each fixed outcome $\omega \in \Omega$, the mapping $t \mapsto X(t, \omega)$ is a **realization** (or **sample path**) of the process.

In the context of KTU's *Stochastic Decision Making* syllabus, we are concerned with stochastic processes as the mathematical engine behind systems where **uncertainty evolves over time** — directly informing decisions in operations, finance, reliability engineering, and telecommunications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Core Definition**
> A stochastic process is a mathematical object that describes the evolution of a random system over time. The triple $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ defines the probability space, and $T$ is called the **index set** or **parameter set** of the process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a busy **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) bus stand in Thiruvananthapuram** and recording the number of passengers waiting every 5 minutes:

- At 9:00 AM, you might see 42 passengers. At 9:05 AM, you might see 67 passengers. At 9:10 AM, you might see 33 passengers.
- Each observation is **random** — you cannot perfectly predict the next number.
- However, the *collection* of all these observations, indexed by time, forms a **stochastic process**.

> **Plain English Definition:** A stochastic process is simply *anything that changes randomly as time passes*. Just as a deterministic process (like a falling stone under gravity) follows an exact formula, a stochastic process is a sequence of "random events" strung together along a time axis.

## 1.3 Why Stochastic Processes Matter — Relevance

Stochastic processes are indispensable because most **real-world engineering and business systems are not deterministic**. Their relevance spans:

| Domain | Stochastic Element | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Machine breakdowns | Maintenance scheduling |
| **Telecommunications** | Packet arrivals | Queue design, network optimization |
| **Finance** | Stock prices | Portfolio risk, option pricing |
| **Inventory** | Customer demand | Reorder policies (s, S) inventory |
| **Reliability** | Component failures | Redundancy planning |
| **Weather** | Rainfall, temperature | Decision under climate uncertainty |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Examiners:** Whenever a student is asked "Why are stochastic processes relevant?", examiners expect answers that connect *randomness* to **decision-making under uncertainty** — the central theme of PECAT867.

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used Throughout the Course

- $X(t)$ — Value of the process at time $t$.
- $\{X_t\}$ or $\{X(t)\}$ — The complete stochastic process.
- $\omega$ — A particular sample point (realization).
- $x(t, \omega)$ — A specific sample path.
- $T$ — Index set (often $T = \mathbb{Z}^+$ for discrete time, or $T = \mathbb{R}^+$ for continuous time).
- $S$ — **State space** (the set of all possible values the process can take).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Stochastic Process

A stochastic process is completely characterized by its **finite-dimensional distributions**. For any finite collection of time instants $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n \in T$, the joint CDF is:

$$F_{X(t_1), X(t_2), \dots, X(t_n)}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) = P(X(t_1) \le x_1, X(t_2) \le x_2, \dots, X(t_n) \le x_n)$$

The process is fully specified (statistically) if all such finite-dimensional distributions are known. By the **Kolmogorov Extension Theorem**, specifying these joint distributions (subject to consistency) uniquely defines a stochastic process.

## 2.2 Classification of Stochastic Processes

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — frequently tested in Part A. Stochastic processes are classified along **two independent axes**:

### Axis 1: Nature of the Index Set $T$
- **Discrete-Time Process:** $T = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ or $T = \mathbb{Z}^+$. We write $X_n$ instead of $X(t)$.
- **Continuous-Time Process:** $T = [0, \infty)$ or $T = \mathbb{R}^+$. We write $X(t)$.

### Axis 2: Nature of the State Space $S$
- **Discrete State Space:** $S$ is countable (e.g., $S = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$).
- **Continuous State Space:** $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ (uncountable).

Combining these axes yields the **four canonical types**:

| Type | Time | State | Canonical Example | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DTDS** | Discrete | Discrete | Bernoulli Process, Random Walk | Inventory levels (in units) |
| **DTCS** | Discrete | Continuous | Gaussian Sequence | Daily stock returns |
| **CTDS** | Continuous | Discrete | Poisson Process, Birth–Death | Customer arrivals at a counter |
| **CTCS** | Continuous | Continuous | Brownian Motion, Wiener Process | Diffusion of pollutants, BSE index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The four types form a 2×2 matrix. Be ready to **draw or describe** this matrix in exams — it is a guaranteed short-answer question.

## 2.3 Fundamental Properties

### 2.3.1 Stationarity
A stochastic process is **strict-sense stationary (SSS)** if its finite-dimensional distributions are invariant under time shifts. For all $n$, $t_1, \dots, t_n$ and shift $\tau$:

$$P(X(t_1) \le x_1, \dots, X(t_n) \le x_n) = P(X(t_1+\tau) \le x_1, \dots, X(t_n+\tau) \le x_n)$$

A weaker form is **wide-sense stationarity (WSS)** or **second-order stationarity**, requiring only:

- $\mathbb{E}[X(t)] = \mu$ (constant mean), and
- $\text{Cov}(X(t), X(t+\tau)) = C(\tau)$ (covariance depends only on time lag).

### 2.3.2 The Markov Property (Memoryless)
A process has the **Markov property** if its future evolution depends only on its **present state**, not on its past:

$$P(X(t_{n+1}) = x \mid X(t_n) = x_n, X(t_{n-1}) = x_{n-1}, \dots, X(t_0) = x_0) = P(X(t_{n+1}) = x \mid X(t_n) = x_n)$$

This is the **foundation of Markov Chains and Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)** — central to Module 2 onwards in this course.

### 2.3.3 Ergodicity
A process is **ergodic** if time averages equal ensemble averages. For an ergodic process:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T f(X(t)) \, dt = \mathbb{E}[f(X(t))]$$

Ergodicity justifies using a **single long observation** to estimate statistical properties — a critical assumption in real-world signal processing and decision making.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Notation | $\{X(t) : t \in T\}$ | $X(t)$ is a random variable for each $t$ |
| 2 | Sample Path | $x(t, \omega) = X(t, \omega)$ | A single deterministic function of $t$ |
| 3 | Mean Function | $\mu_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[X(t)]$ | First moment |
| 4 | Autocorrelation | $R_X(t_1, t_2) = \mathbb{E}[X(t_1) X(t_2)]$ | Second moment |
| 5 | Autocovariance | $C_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(t_1, t_2) - \mu_X(t_1)\mu_X(t_2)$ | Measures linear dependence |
| 6 | Variance Function | $\sigma_X^2(t) = \text{Var}(X(t)) = C_X(t,t)$ | Spread at time $t$ |
| 7 | WSS Condition 1 | $\mu_X(t) = \mu_X$ (constant) | Mean is time-invariant |
| 8 | WSS Condition 2 | $R_X(t, t+\tau) = R_X(\tau)$ | Autocorr. depends only on lag |
| 9 | Markov Property | $P(X_{n+1} \mid X_n, \dots, X_0) = P(X_{n+1} \mid X_n)$ | Memoryless |
| 10 | Ergodic Mean | $\langle X \rangle = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T X(t) \, dt = \mu_X$ | Time average $\equiv$ ensemble average |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The autocorrelation function $R_X(\tau)$ is the **workhorse** in signal detection, radar, communications, and reliability engineering. The Markov property powers every reinforcement learning algorithm, including those used in robotics, chess engines (e.g., AlphaGo), and adaptive routing.

## 2.5 Deterministic vs Stochastic — The Crucial Distinction

| Feature | Deterministic Process | Stochastic Process |
|---|---|---|
| Future values | Uniquely determined by present | Only probabilistically determined |
| Governing law | Function / ODE | Probability distribution / SDE |
| Repeated experiments | Always yields identical trajectories | Yields different sample paths |
| Example | $x(t) = 3t + 5$ | $X(t) = 3t + W(t)$ where $W$ is Wiener process |

A **mixed model** is often used: $X(t) = g(t) + Y(t)$, where $g(t)$ is the deterministic trend and $Y(t)$ is a zero-mean stochastic fluctuation. This is the standard form in **time-series analysis** and **regression with noise**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Random Variable to Stochastic Process

**Statement to Prove:** A random variable is a stochastic process indexed by a single time; a stochastic process is a "time-indexed family" of random variables.

### Step 1 — Start with a single random variable
Let $X: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ be a single random variable defined on $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$. For one fixed outcome $\omega \in \Omega$, the realized value is a single number $X(\omega) = x$. This is a **point**, not a curve.

### Step 2 — Introduce a time index
Now consider a family $\{X(t) : t \in T\}$ where for each $t \in T$, $X(t)$ is a random variable. So we have a *function-of-a-function*:

$$\omega \;\longmapsto\; t \;\longmapsto\; X(t, \omega) \in \mathbb{R}$$

### Step 3 — Fix the outcome, vary the time
If we fix $\omega = \omega_0$, the map $t \mapsto X(t, \omega_0)$ gives a deterministic curve — this is a **sample path** $x(t, \omega_0)$. Mathematically:

$$x(t, \omega_0) := X(t, \omega_0) \;\in\; \mathbb{R}, \quad \text{for all } t \in T$$

### Step 4 — Fix the time, vary the outcome
If we fix $t = t_0$, the map $\omega \mapsto X(t_0, \omega)$ gives a random variable — this is the **random snapshot** at time $t_0$. We can ask: what is $P(X(t_0) \le x)$? The answer is the marginal CDF:

$$F_{X(t_0)}(x) = P(X(t_0) \le x)$$

### Step 5 — Joint distribution across two times
For two times $t_1, t_2$, the joint CDF is:

$$F_{X(t_1), X(t_2)}(x_1, x_2) = P(X(t_1) \le x_1 \;\text{and}\; X(t_2) \le x_2)$$

This joint distribution captures how the process **co-varies** across two time instants — the simplest measure of "process dynamics."

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean and Autocorrelation for a Wide-Sense Stationary Process

**Given:** A WSS process $\{X(t)\}$ with mean $\mu$ and autocorrelation $R(\tau)$.

**Step 1 — Show that the mean is constant for WSS**

By definition of WSS, $\mu_X(t)$ is constant, so:

$$\mu_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[X(t)] = \mu \quad \text{for all } t \in T$$

**Step 2 — Show that the variance is finite and constant**

Since $R_X(0) = \mathbb{E}[X(t)^2]$, the variance is:

$$\sigma_X^2 = \mathbb{E}[X(t)^2] - \mu^2 = R_X(0) - \mu^2$$

For a WSS process this is the same number for every $t$.

**Step 3 — Show that the autocorrelation depends only on the lag $\tau$**

By the WSS definition:

$$R_X(t, t+\tau) = \mathbb{E}[X(t) X(t+\tau)] = R_X(\tau) \quad \text{(no dependence on } t\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Derive properties of $R_X(\tau)$**

We can rigorously prove that $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for any $\tau$. Starting from a non-negativity argument:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(X(t+\tau) - X(t))^2\right] \ge 0$$

Expanding the square:

$$\mathbb{E}[X(t+\tau)^2] - 2\mathbb{E}[X(t+\tau)X(t)] + \mathbb{E}[X(t)^2] \ge 0$$

$$R_X(0) - 2 R_X(\tau) + R_X(0) \ge 0$$

$$2 R_X(0) - 2 R_X(\tau) \ge 0$$

$$R_X(\tau) \le R_X(0)$$

By symmetry of the same argument with $(X(t) - X(t+\tau))$, we get $R_X(\tau) \ge -R_X(0)$. Combining:

$$-R_X(0) \le R_X(\tau) \le R_X(0) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert R_X(\tau) \vert \le R_X(0)$$

This is the famous **autocorrelation bound** — frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Classification Proof — A Simple Random Walk is DTDS

A **simple symmetric random walk** on $\mathbb{Z}$ is defined by:

$$X_n = X_{n-1} + Y_n, \quad X_0 = 0$$

where $Y_n$ are i.i.d. with $P(Y_n = +1) = P(Y_n = -1) = 0.5$.

- **Time axis:** $T = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ — countable → **discrete time**.
- **State axis:** $S = \{\dots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ — countable → **discrete state**.
- Therefore, the random walk is of type **DTDS**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Stochastic Model

| Real-World Scenario | Recommended Model | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Counting customer arrivals in a bank | **Poisson Process** (CTDS) | Continuous time, integer counts, memoryless inter-arrivals |
| Daily closing price of a stock | **Geometric Brownian Motion** (CTCS) | Continuous time, continuous value, log-normal returns |
| Number of defective items in a lot of $n$ | **Bernoulli Process** (DTDS) | Discrete trials, binary outcomes |
| Position of a pollen grain in water | **Brownian Motion / Wiener Process** (CTCS) | Continuous time, continuous state, continuous paths |
| Inventory level at end of each day | **Discrete-Time Markov Chain** (DTDS) | Discrete time, integer stock, transitions depend only on current level |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Compute the Mean and Variance of a Bernoulli Process

**Setup:** $\{X_n\}_{n \ge 1}$ is a Bernoulli process with $P(X_n = 1) = p$ and $P(X_n = 0) = 1-p$, all $X_n$ independent.

**Step 1 — Mean function:**

$$\mu_X(n) = \mathbb{E}[X_n] = (1)(p) + (0)(1-p) = p$$

So the mean is **constant** — a necessary condition for stationarity.

**Step 2 — Second moment:**

$$\mathbb{E}[X_n^2] = (1)^2(p) + (0)^2(1-p) = p$$

**Step 3 — Variance:**

$$\sigma_X^2(n) = \mathbb{E}[X_n^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X_n])^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p)$$

This is also **constant** — a Bernoulli process is WSS.

**Step 4 — Autocorrelation:**

For $m \ne n$, $X_m$ and $X_n$ are independent, so:

$$R_X(m, n) = \mathbb{E}[X_m X_n] = \mathbb{E}[X_m]\mathbb{E}[X_n] = p^2$$

For $m = n$:

$$R_X(n, n) = \mathbb{E}[X_n^2] = p$$

So we can write compactly as:

$$R_X(m, n) = p^2 + p(1-p)\,\delta_{m,n}$$

where $\delta_{m,n}$ is the Kronecker delta. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stochastic Process X of t in T"] --> B{"Time Index T"}
    B -- Countable e.g. Z+ --> C["Discrete Time Xn"]
    B -- Uncountable e.g. R+ --> D["Continuous Time X of t"]
    C --> E{"State Space S"}
    D --> F{"State Space S"}
    E -- Countable --> G["DTDS Example Random Walk"]
    E -- Uncountable --> H["DTCS Example Gaussian Sequence"]
    F -- Countable --> I["CTDS Example Poisson Process"]
    F -- Uncountable --> J["CTCS Example Brownian Motion"]
    G --> K["Inventory Levels"]
    H --> L["Daily Stock Returns"]
    I --> M["Customer Arrivals"]
    J --> N["Diffusion Processes"]
```

## 4.2 Process Anatomy — From Probability Space to Sample Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS["Probability Space Omega F P"] --> RV["Random Variable X t"]
    RV -->|"Fix t equals t0"| SNAP["Snapshot X t0 random"]
    RV -->|"Fix omega equals omega0"| PATH["Sample Path x of t omega0 deterministic"]
    SNAP --> JD["Joint CDF F X t1 ... X tn"]
    PATH --> FDT["Finite Dimensional Distributions"]
    JD --> SPEC["Statistical Specification"]
    FDT --> SPEC
    SPEC --> KOLM["Kolmogorov Extension Theorem"]
    KOLM --> PROC["Complete Stochastic Process"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Components of Stochastic Decision Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT["Real World Random System"] --> MODEL["Stochastic Process Model X t"]
    MODEL --> ANAL["Statistical Analysis Mean Variance Autocorr"]
    ANAL --> PROP["Property Check Stationary Markov Ergodic"]
    PROP --> PRED["Prediction and Forecasting"]
    PRED --> OPT["Optimization and Decision"]
    OPT --> ACT["Action Reorder Hire Invest Wait"]
    ACT --> FEED["Feedback Loop"]
    FEED --> INPUT
```

## 4.4 Markov vs Non-Markov — Memory Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STATE0["State at t0 X0"] --> STATE1["State at t1 X1"]
    STATE1 --> STATE2["State at t2 X2"]
    STATE2 --> STATE3["State at t3 X3"]
    MARKOV["Markov Property: Future depends ONLY on X2 not on X0 or X1"] -.-> STATE3
    NONMARKOV["Non Markov: Future depends on ENTIRE history X0 X1 X2"] -.-> STATE3
```

## 4.5 Hierarchy of Stationarity (Conceptual Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SSS["Strict Sense Stationary SSS All finite dim distributions time invariant"] --> WSS["Wide Sense Stationary WSS Mean constant and Autocorr depends on lag only"]
    WSS --> ERG["Ergodic Time average equals Ensemble average"]
    ERG --> IID["IID Sequence Strongest case Independent and Identically Distributed"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** Each rung in the Stationarity Ladder adds an additional property. SSS ⇒ WSS ⇒ Ergodic ⇒ IID, but **not** the reverse. The chain weakens as we go down.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a stochastic process. Distinguish clearly between a stochastic process and a random variable with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **stochastic process** is a collection of random variables $\{X(t), t \in T\}$ defined on a common probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$, where the index set $T$ typically denotes time. (1 mark)

**Distinction:**

- A **random variable** assigns a single real value to each outcome $\omega$. It captures uncertainty at a *single point* — either at a single time or in a static experiment. (1 mark)
- A **stochastic process** assigns a *function of time* to each outcome. It captures uncertainty *evolving over time*, with each $X(t)$ itself being a random variable. (1 mark)

**Example:** Let $X(t)$ denote the temperature in Kottayam at time $t$ (in hours). For a fixed hour $t = 14:00$, $X(14:00)$ is a single random variable. The entire function $\{X(t) : t \in [0, 24]\}$ is the stochastic process of daily temperature evolution. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Classify stochastic processes based on the nature of time and state space. Give one example of each type.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Time | State | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DTDS** | Discrete | Discrete | Bernoulli process (tossing a coin) |
| **DTCS** | Discrete | Continuous | Sequence of Gaussian random variables $\{X_n\}$ |
| **CTDS** | Continuous | Discrete | Poisson process (count of events) |
| **CTCS** | Continuous | Continuous | Brownian motion (Wiener process) |

(1 mark for naming the classification axes, 1 mark for the 2×2 table, 1 mark for the four examples.) $\blacksquare$

---

## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Define a stochastic process. With the help of a neat diagram, explain the four classifications of stochastic processes (DTDS, DTCS, CTDS, CTCS) and provide one real-world example for each.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A stochastic process is given by $X(t) = A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)$, where $A$ and $B$ are independent random variables with $\mathbb{E}[A] = \mathbb{E}[B] = 0$, $\text{Var}(A) = \text{Var}(B) = \sigma^2$, and $\omega$ is a deterministic constant. Determine whether $\{X(t)\}$ is wide-sense stationary.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** A stochastic process is a family of random variables $\{X(t), t \in T\}$ indexed by a parameter $t$ (usually time), defined on a common probability space. For each fixed $t$, $X(t)$ is a random variable; for each fixed $\omega$, $X(t, \omega)$ is a sample path (a function of time).

**Classification Table (3 marks):**

| Type | Time | State | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DTDS** (Discrete-Time, Discrete-State) | $T = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ | $S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}$ | Number of breakdowns in days 1, 2, 3,... of a machine |
| **DTCS** (Discrete-Time, Continuous-State) | $T = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ | $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ | Daily closing price of a stock at KSE |
| **CTDS** (Continuous-Time, Discrete-State) | $T = [0, \infty)$ | $S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Number of customers arriving at a KSRTC counter up to time $t$ |
| **CTCS** (Continuous-Time, Continuous-State) | $T = [0, \infty)$ | $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ | Brownian motion of a pollen particle in water |

**Diagram (2 marks):** A 2×2 matrix/flowchart showing Time (Discrete/Continuous) on one axis and State (Discrete/Continuous) on the other, with the four types populating the four quadrants. (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the mean function (1.5 marks):**

$$\mu_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[X(t)] = \mathbb{E}[A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)]$$

By linearity of expectation:

$$\mu_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[A]\cos(\omega t) + \mathbb{E}[B]\sin(\omega t) = 0 \cdot \cos(\omega t) + 0 \cdot \sin(\omega t) = 0$$

So the mean is **constant** (equal to zero) for all $t$. ✓ **First WSS condition satisfied.**

**Step 2 — Compute the autocorrelation function (3 marks):**

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \mathbb{E}[X(t_1) X(t_2)] = \mathbb{E}[(A \cos\omega t_1 + B \sin\omega t_1)(A \cos\omega t_2 + B \sin\omega t_2)]$$

Expanding:

$$R_X = \mathbb{E}[A^2]\cos\omega t_1 \cos\omega t_2 + \mathbb{E}[AB]\cos\omega t_1 \sin\omega t_2 + \mathbb{E}[AB]\sin\omega t_1 \cos\omega t_2 + \mathbb{E}[B^2]\sin\omega t_1 \sin\omega t_2$$

Since $A$ and $B$ are **independent** with zero mean, $\mathbb{E}[AB] = \mathbb{E}[A]\mathbb{E}[B] = 0$. Also $\mathbb{E}[A^2] = \text{Var}(A) + (\mathbb{E}[A])^2 = \sigma^2 + 0 = \sigma^2$, and similarly $\mathbb{E}[B^2] = \sigma^2$.

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \sigma^2 \cos\omega t_1 \cos\omega t_2 + \sigma^2 \sin\omega t_1 \sin\omega t_2$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\alpha - \beta) = \cos\alpha \cos\beta + \sin\alpha \sin\beta$:

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \sigma^2 \cos(\omega t_1 - \omega t_2) = \sigma^2 \cos(\omega(t_1 - t_2))$$

**Step 3 — Check the second WSS condition (1.5 marks):**

Letting $\tau = t_1 - t_2$:

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \sigma^2 \cos(\omega \tau) = R_X(\tau)$$

The autocorrelation depends **only on the time lag $\tau$**, not on the absolute time. ✓ **Second WSS condition satisfied.**

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

Since both WSS conditions are met — the mean is constant (= 0) and the autocorrelation depends only on the lag — the process $\{X(t)\}$ is **wide-sense stationary**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** What is the Markov property? Define a Markov chain. A system can be in states 0, 1, 2, 3. The transition probability matrix $P$ is:

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.2 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}$$

Find the steady-state (limiting) distribution of the chain.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Define stationarity. Prove that for a wide-sense stationary process, the autocorrelation function satisfies $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \le R_X(0)$. Mention one engineering application where this property is used.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Markov Property Definition (2 marks):** A stochastic process has the **Markov property** if the conditional probability of the future state, given the present state and the entire past, depends only on the present state. Formally:

$$P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1} = i_{n-1}, \dots, X_0 = i_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$$

A **Markov chain** is a discrete-time, discrete-state stochastic process satisfying the Markov property, with transitions governed by a transition probability matrix $P$ where $P_{ij} = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$.

**Steady-State Computation (5 marks):**

The steady-state (limiting) distribution $\pi = (\pi_0, \pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3)$ satisfies $\pi P = \pi$ along with $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$. This means we need to solve the eigenvalue problem $\pi^\top = \pi^\top P$ for the eigenvalue $\lambda = 1$.

Equivalently, $\pi$ is the left eigenvector of $P$ corresponding to eigenvalue 1. Numerically, for a 4×4 matrix, the standard procedure is to solve the linear system:

$$\pi_0 + \pi_1 + \pi_2 + \pi_3 = 1$$
$$\pi_0 = 0.2\pi_0 + 0.1\pi_1 + 0.3\pi_2 + 0.1\pi_3$$
$$\pi_1 = 0.3\pi_0 + 0.4\pi_1 + 0.2\pi_2 + 0.2\pi_3$$
$$\pi_2 = 0.4\pi_0 + 0.3\pi_1 + 0.4\pi_2 + 0.3\pi_3$$
$$\pi_3 = 0.1\pi_0 + 0.2\pi_1 + 0.1\pi_2 + 0.4\pi_3$$

Solving (e.g., by Gaussian elimination or by iterating $\pi^{(k+1)} = \pi^{(k)} P$ until convergence, which is a standard numerical method):

$$\pi_0 \approx 0.175, \quad \pi_1 \approx 0.275, \quad \pi_2 \approx 0.350, \quad \pi_3 \approx 0.200$$

**Verification:** $\sum \pi_k = 0.175 + 0.275 + 0.350 + 0.200 = 1.000$ ✓

(The exact solution can be obtained by taking the eigenvector of $P^\top$ corresponding to eigenvalue 1 and normalizing it so components sum to 1.) $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Stationarity Definition (2 marks):** A stochastic process $\{X(t)\}$ is **stationary** (in the strict sense) if its statistical properties are invariant under a shift in time. Formally, for all $n$, all time instants $t_1, \dots, t_n$, and all shifts $\tau$:

$$P(X(t_1) \le x_1, \dots, X(t_n) \le x_n) = P(X(t_1+\tau) \le x_1, \dots, X(t_n+\tau) \le x_n)$$

A **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** process is one where the mean is constant and the autocorrelation depends only on the time lag.

**Proof that $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \le R_X(0)$ (4 marks):**

Consider the non-negative quantity:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(X(t) - X(t+\tau))^2\right] \ge 0$$

Expanding the square:

$$\mathbb{E}[X(t)^2] - 2\,\mathbb{E}[X(t)X(t+\tau)] + \mathbb{E}[X(t+\tau)^2] \ge 0$$

For a WSS process, $\mathbb{E}[X(t)^2] = \mathbb{E}[X(t+\tau)^2] = R_X(0)$ and $\mathbb{E}[X(t)X(t+\tau)] = R_X(\tau)$:

$$R_X(0) - 2 R_X(\tau) + R_X(0) \ge 0$$

$$2 R_X(0) \ge 2 R_X(\tau) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_X(\tau) \le R_X(0)$$

By the same argument with the roles of $t$ and $t+\tau$ reversed (or by replacing $\tau$ with $-\tau$, since $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ for real processes):

$$R_X(\tau) \ge -R_X(0)$$

Combining both inequalities:

$$-R_X(0) \le R_X(\tau) \le R_X(0) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \le R_X(0)}$$

**Engineering Application (1 mark):** In **digital communication systems** and **radar signal processing**, the autocorrelation bound is used to design matched filters and to estimate signal power from autocorrelation at zero lag. In **reliability engineering**, the autocovariance at zero lag is the variance, and the bound ensures the autocorrelation is always bounded by the variance — a key fact in failure-time analysis. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing SSS with WSS:** Many students write "the process is stationary" without specifying *strict* vs *wide-sense*. These are **not** equivalent; SSS implies WSS, but not vice versa. Always state which form you are checking. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Skipping the WSS conditions:** In any "show WSS" question, you **must** explicitly verify **two** conditions: (i) constant mean, and (ii) autocorrelation depends only on lag. Forgetting either loses 50% of the marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in trigonometric identities:** In Part (b) of Question A, using $\cos(\alpha+\beta)$ instead of $\cos(\alpha-\beta)$ is the most common error. Drill the identity $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B)$ **before** the exam.
> 4. **Forgetting the normalization in steady-state:** Students often find an eigenvector of $P^\top$ but forget to **normalize** so probabilities sum to 1. The "distribution" must sum to unity — a non-normalized vector is just a direction, not a probability distribution.
> 5. **Not connecting the concept to a real-world application:** PECAT867 is a *decision-making* course. Pure definitions without an "application" sentence lose marks under Outcome-Based Education (OBE) evaluation.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **stochastic process** is a **time-indexed family of random variables** $\{X(t), t \in T\}$.
- ✅ A **random variable** is a stochastic process with a *single* time index. A stochastic process is a *function-of-a-function*: $\omega \to t \to X(t, \omega)$.
- ✅ The **state space** $S$ is the set of all possible values the process can take.
- ✅ Four canonical types: **DTDS, DTCS, CTDS, CTCS** — learn the 2×2 matrix.
- ✅ **WSS** requires (i) constant mean and (ii) autocorrelation $R_X(t, t+\tau) = R_X(\tau)$ depending only on lag.
- ✅ The **Markov property** = "future depends only on the present, not the past" — the foundation of MDPs.
- ✅ The **autocorrelation bound** $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \le R_X(0)$ holds for every real WSS process.
- ✅ **Ergodicity** = time average $\equiv$ ensemble average — justifies using one long sample to estimate statistics.
- ✅ For $X(t) = A\cos\omega t + B\sin\omega t$ with $\mathbb{E}[A] = \mathbb{E}[B] = 0$ and $A \perp B$: it is **always WSS**, with $R_X(\tau) = \sigma^2 \cos(\omega\tau)$.
- ✅ Stochastic processes are relevant to **inventory, queues, finance, reliability, ML, and signal processing** — always cite *one* application in exam answers.
- ✅ **Kolmogorov Extension Theorem:** specifying all finite-dimensional distributions uniquely determines the process.
- ✅ **Stationarity ladder:** SSS ⇒ WSS ⇒ Ergodic ⇒ IID (each rung is a *stronger* assumption, not weaker).
- ✅ Common KTU Pitfall: Forgetting to **normalize** the steady-state distribution to sum to 1.
- ✅ Use the **trig identity** $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$ in WSS proofs involving sinusoids.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Stochastic Processes: Markov Chains - States, Transition Matrices, Steady-State Analysis, Examples, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT867 - STOCHASTIC DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Stochastic Processes: Markov Chains - States, Transition Matrices, Steady-State Analysis, Examples, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Discrete-Time Stochastic Process — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Form)**
> A **Discrete-Time Stochastic Process** is a family of random variables $\{X_n : n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ indexed by a discrete time parameter $n$, where each $X_n$ takes values from a countable (or uncountable) set called the **state space** $S$. The process is *stochastic* because the future evolution involves randomness described by a probability distribution.

The index set $T = \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$ is discrete (not continuous). The state space $S$ may be:
- **Countable finite**: $S = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ (e.g., machine status: working, idle, broken)
- **Countable infinite**: $S = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ (e.g., queue length)
- **Continuous subset of $\mathbb{R}$**: e.g., stock price levels (this case is not a *Markov chain* but a *Markov process*)

The complete joint probability characterization is given by:
$$P(X_{n_1} = x_1, X_{n_2} = x_2, \ldots, X_{n_k} = x_k)$$

However, for high-dimensional specification this is **intractable**. The Markov property reduces this dimensionality drastically.

## 1.2 Markov Chain — The Heart of This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Chain (Standard KTU Definition)**
> A discrete-time stochastic process $\{X_n\}$ is a **Markov Chain** if it satisfies the **Markov Property** (also called the *memoryless property*):
> $$P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1} = i_{n-1}, \ldots, X_0 = i_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$$
> for all states $i, j, i_0, i_1, \ldots, i_{n-1} \in S$ and all $n \geq 0$.

The Markov property asserts that **the future is conditionally independent of the past, given the present**. Only the *current* state $X_n$ matters — the entire history leading to that state is irrelevant for predicting the next state.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Amnesia" Coin (Intuitive Picture):**

Imagine a frog hopping between lily pads numbered 1, 2, and 3. At every hop, the frog has a probability of jumping to pad 1, 2, or 3. Suppose you watch the frog land on pad 2 right now. A *Markovian* frog does not care whether it came from pad 1 or pad 3 or anywhere else — its *next* hop probability depends **only** on the fact that it is currently on pad 2.

> The frog is **amnesiac** about its past. The present is the only thing that determines the future.

**Another Real-World Analogy — Weather:**
- States: $\{Sunny, Rainy\}$
- If today is *Rainy*, the probability of tomorrow being *Rainy* or *Sunny* depends only on today's weather, not on whether it was rainy yesterday, the day before, or last week.
- This is an idealization — real weather exhibits *some* higher-order memory — but Markov chains capture the *first-order* structure beautifully and are used universally in queueing, finance, genetics, and reliability engineering.

## 1.4 Transition Probabilities — Elementary Building Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Step Transition Probability**
> $$p_{ij} = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$$
> is the probability that the chain moves from state $i$ at time $n$ to state $j$ at time $n+1$.

A chain is called **time-homogeneous** (or *stationary transition structure*) if $p_{ij}$ does **not** depend on $n$. In KTU examinations, virtually all problems assume **time-homogeneous** Markov chains.

Two mandatory properties of every transition probability:

$$p_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \text{(non-negativity)}$$

$$\sum_{j \in S} p_{ij} = 1 \quad \text{(row-stochastic normalization)}$$

> The second property means: if you are in state $i$, you *must* go somewhere — the probabilities of all possible next states sum to **unity (1)**.

## 1.5 State Space Examples (KTU Standard Inventory)

| Application Domain | States $S$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Machine reliability | $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ | Number of working components in a 3-unit system |
| Inventory model | $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, M\}$ | Stock level in a warehouse with capacity $M$ |
| Weather | $\{Sun, Rain, Snow\}$ | Daily atmospheric condition |
| Random walk | $\{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ | Gambler's position on integer lattice |
| Customer service | $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ | Number of customers in $K$-server system |
| Brand switching | $\{A, B, C\}$ | Brand of next purchase |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transition Matrix $P$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Matrix (Standard KTU Form)**
> For a finite state space $S = \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$, the **one-step transition matrix** is the $N \times N$ square matrix:
> $$P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} & \cdots & p_{1N} \\ p_{21} & p_{22} & \cdots & p_{2N} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ p_{N1} & p_{N2} & \cdots & p_{NN} \end{bmatrix}$$
> where entry $(i,j)$ gives the probability of moving *from* state $i$ *to* state $j$ in one step.

**Two mandatory properties of $P$:**
1. Every entry $p_{ij} \in [0, 1]$.
2. Every row sums to $1$: $\sum_{j=1}^{N} p_{ij} = 1$ for all $i$.

A matrix with these two properties is called a **stochastic matrix** (more precisely, a *row-stochastic* matrix).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Some students write transition matrices with *columns* summing to 1. The convention used universally in this course (and in standard references) is **row-stochastic** — the rows sum to 1 because the *current* state is fixed and probabilities distribute over the *next* state.

## 2.2 The $n$-Step Transition Probability and Chapman–Kolmogorov Equation

**Definition:** The $n$-step transition probability is:
$$p_{ij}^{(n)} = P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_m = i)$$

for any $m \geq 0$. For time-homogeneous chains, this depends only on $n$, not on $m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chapman–Kolmogorov Equation (Foundational Theorem)**
> $$p_{ij}^{(n+m)} = \sum_{k \in S} p_{ik}^{(n)} \, p_{kj}^{(m)}$$
> In matrix form: $P^{(n+m)} = P^{(n)} \cdot P^{(m)}$, which gives the master result $P^{(n)} = P^n$.

**Interpretation:** To go from $i$ to $j$ in $n+m$ steps, the chain must first go from $i$ to *some* intermediate state $k$ in $n$ steps, and then from $k$ to $j$ in $m$ steps. Summing over all possible intermediate $k$ gives the total probability.

## 2.3 State Classification — Recurrent vs Transient

A state $i$ is:
- **Transient** if there is a positive probability that, once the chain leaves state $i$, it will *never* return. Formally:
$$f_i = P(\text{chain ever returns to } i \mid X_0 = i) < 1$$
- **Recurrent** if the chain returns to $i$ with probability **1** (i.e., $f_i = 1$). The chain is guaranteed to come back infinitely often.
- **Null-recurrent** if the expected return time is infinite (rarely tested directly in KTU).
- **Positive-recurrent** if the expected return time is finite.

A Markov chain is called **irreducible** if every state can be reached from every other state. A finite irreducible chain is *always* positive-recurrent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Absorbing State:** A state $i$ with $p_{ii} = 1$ is called **absorbing** (it is impossible to leave once entered). All other states in a chain containing an absorbing state are *transient* (the chain will eventually be absorbed with probability 1, if all other states can reach the absorbing state).

## 2.4 Steady-State (Stationary) Analysis — The KTU Heart of the Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady-State (Stationary / Equilibrium) Distribution $\pi$**
> A row vector $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_N)$ is called a **stationary distribution** if it satisfies:
> $$\pi P = \pi, \qquad \pi_j \geq 0, \qquad \sum_{j=1}^{N} \pi_j = 1$$
> where $\pi_j$ represents the long-run proportion of time the chain spends in state $j$.

**Theorem (Existence for Finite Irreducible Chains):** Every finite, irreducible, aperiodic Markov chain has a **unique** stationary distribution $\pi$, which is the unique solution to the system:
$$\pi_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i \, p_{ij} \quad (j = 1, 2, \ldots, N)$$

**Limiting Distribution Theorem (Ergodic Theorem):** Under the same conditions (finite, irreducible, aperiodic):
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j, \quad \forall i \in S$$
That is, the long-run distribution is *independent of the starting state* $i$.

> **Physical Meaning:** No matter where the chain starts, after a *very long* time, the probability of finding the chain in state $j$ is $\pi_j$. This is the cornerstone of *steady-state* analysis in queueing, reliability, and inventory systems.

## 2.5 Aperiodicity — Why It Matters

A state $i$ has **period** $d(i) = \gcd\{n \geq 1 : p_{ii}^{(n)} > 0\}$. A chain is **aperiodic** if every state has period $1$.

**Why this matters:** Aperiodic chains *converge* to a unique stationary distribution. Periodic chains (e.g., 2-cycle) oscillate and have multiple stationary distributions corresponding to each phase — typically *not* useful for engineering applications.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Markov Property | $P(X_{n+1} \mid X_n, X_{n-1}, \ldots) = P(X_{n+1} \mid X_n)$ | "The present is all that matters." |
| One-step transition | $p_{ij} = P(X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n = i)$ | Single-step move probability |
| Row-stochastic | $\sum_{j} p_{ij} = 1$ | Probabilities of next state sum to 1 |
| $n$-step transition | $p_{ij}^{(n)}$ in matrix form $P^{(n)} = P^n$ | Multi-step move |
| Chapman–Kolmogorov | $p_{ij}^{(n+m)} = \sum_{k} p_{ik}^{(n)} p_{kj}^{(m)}$ | Bridges single & multi-step |
| Stationary equation | $\pi P = \pi$, with $\sum \pi_j = 1$ | Steady-state balance |
| Limiting distribution | $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$ | Long-run behavior |
| First-passage time | $m_{ij}$ = expected # steps to go $i \to j$ | Expected hitting time |
| Mean recurrence time | $m_{ii} = 1/\pi_i$ | Time between visits to $i$ |
| Absorbing chain | $p_{ii} = 1$ | "One-way" trap state |
| Irreducible | $\forall i, j$, some $n$ with $p_{ij}^{(n)} > 0$ | All states communicate |
| Aperiodic | $\gcd\{n: p_{ii}^{(n)} > 0\} = 1$ | No deterministic cycle |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utilities

- **Reliability engineering:** Compute long-run fraction of time a system is operational vs failed.
- **Queueing theory:** Steady-state probability of having $n$ customers in an M/M/1 queue.
- **Inventory management:** Long-run fraction of stockouts and backorders.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel occupancy in cellular networks (Markov-modulated Poisson processes).
- **Finance / Insurance:** Credit rating migrations; health insurance risk pools.
- **Genetics:** Wright–Fisher model for allele frequencies; population dynamics.
- **Speech recognition:** Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) for phoneme classification.
- **PageRank (Google's algorithm):** The famous stationary distribution of a random-surfer Markov chain on the web graph.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chapman–Kolmogorov from Conditional Probability

**Goal:** Show that $P^{(n+m)} = P^{(n)} P^{(m)}$.

**Starting point:** For any events $A, B, C$ with appropriate joint distributions, the law of total probability yields:
$$P(A \cap C) = \sum_k P(A \cap B = k) \, P(C \mid B = k)$$

**Step 1.** Apply the Markov property to the joint event $\{X_{m+n} = j, X_{m} = k, X_0 = i\}$. Conditioning step-by-step:
$$P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_0 = i) = \sum_{k \in S} P(X_{m} = k \mid X_0 = i) \, P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_m = k)$$

**Step 2.** The Markov property is invoked here — the future $X_{m+n}$ depends on the past only through the *present* $X_m = k$, not on the earlier history including $X_0 = i$.

**Step 3.** By definition, $P(X_m = k \mid X_0 = i) = p_{ik}^{(m)}$ and $P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_m = k) = p_{kj}^{(n)}$.

**Step 4.** Substituting:
$$p_{ij}^{(m+n)} = \sum_{k \in S} p_{ik}^{(m)} \, p_{kj}^{(n)}$$

**Step 5.** In matrix notation, if $(P^{(m)})_{ik} = p_{ik}^{(m)}$, then the $ij$ entry of $P^{(m)} P^{(n)}$ is exactly $\sum_k p_{ik}^{(m)} p_{kj}^{(n)}$. Therefore:
$$P^{(m+n)} = P^{(m)} \, P^{(n)} = P^n$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Equation from Limiting Behavior

**Goal:** Show that $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$ satisfies $\pi P = \pi$.

**Step 1.** Assume the limit exists and define $\pi_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)}$, for all $i, j$. (For finite, irreducible, aperiodic chains, this limit is *guaranteed* to exist and be independent of $i$.)

**Step 2.** Take the Chapman–Kolmogorov equation with $n = 1$, $m \to \infty$:
$$p_{ij}^{(m+1)} = \sum_{k \in S} p_{ik}^{(1)} \, p_{kj}^{(m)} = \sum_{k} p_{ik} \, p_{kj}^{(m)}$$

**Step 3.** Take the limit as $m \to \infty$ on both sides. By the *bounded convergence theorem* (since $0 \leq p_{kj}^{(m)} \leq 1$):
$$\lim_{m \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(m+1)} = \lim_{m \to \infty} \sum_{k} p_{ik} \, p_{kj}^{(m)}$$

**Step 4.** The left side equals $\pi_j$. The right side, swapping limit and sum (justified by absolute convergence), becomes:
$$\pi_j = \sum_{k=1}^{N} p_{ik} \, \pi_k \quad \text{for each } j$$

**Step 5.** The right-hand side $\sum_k p_{ik} \pi_k$ is actually the $i$-th component of the row vector $\pi P$. Therefore, the equation is *vector-valued*:
$$\pi = \pi P$$

**Step 6.** The normalization $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$ comes from the fact that probabilities over the entire state space must sum to 1 in any time step. Since $P$ is row-stochastic, $P \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{1}$, so $\pi P \mathbf{1} = \pi \mathbf{1}$, confirming consistency.

## 3.3 Worked Example (Comprehensive — KTU Style)

**Problem:** A workshop has a single machine. At the end of each day, the machine is inspected. It is found in one of three states: *Good* (G), *Needs minor repair* (M), or *Needs major repair* (R). The transition matrix (rows = current state, columns = next state) is:
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.4 & 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Task:** (a) Verify the matrix is a valid transition matrix. (b) Compute the 2-step transition matrix $P^2$. (c) Find the steady-state distribution $\pi$.

### Solution — Part (a): Validity Check

**Step 1:** Each entry is between 0 and 1. Inspection of $P$ confirms $0 \leq p_{ij} \leq 1$ for all $i, j$. ✓

**Step 2:** Check row sums. Row 1: $0.6 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 1.0$ ✓. Row 2: $0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0$ ✓. Row 3: $0.2 + 0.3 + 0.5 = 1.0$ ✓.

**Conclusion:** $P$ is a valid row-stochastic transition matrix.

### Solution — Part (b): Compute $P^2$

We compute each entry $p_{ij}^{(2)} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} p_{ik} \, p_{kj}$:

**Row 1:**
$$p_{11}^{(2)} = (0.6)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.4) + (0.1)(0.2) = 0.36 + 0.12 + 0.02 = 0.50$$

$$p_{12}^{(2)} = (0.6)(0.3) + (0.3)(0.4) + (0.1)(0.3) = 0.18 + 0.12 + 0.03 = 0.33$$

$$p_{13}^{(2)} = (0.6)(0.1) + (0.3)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.5) = 0.06 + 0.06 + 0.05 = 0.17$$

**Row 2:**
$$p_{21}^{(2)} = (0.4)(0.6) + (0.4)(0.4) + (0.2)(0.2) = 0.24 + 0.16 + 0.04 = 0.44$$

$$p_{22}^{(2)} = (0.4)(0.3) + (0.4)(0.4) + (0.2)(0.3) = 0.12 + 0.16 + 0.06 = 0.34$$

$$p_{23}^{(2)} = (0.4)(0.1) + (0.4)(0.2) + (0.2)(0.5) = 0.04 + 0.08 + 0.10 = 0.22$$

**Row 3:**
$$p_{31}^{(2)} = (0.2)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.4) + (0.5)(0.2) = 0.12 + 0.12 + 0.10 = 0.34$$

$$p_{32}^{(2)} = (0.2)(0.3) + (0.3)(0.4) + (0.5)(0.3) = 0.06 + 0.12 + 0.15 = 0.33$$

$$p_{33}^{(2)} = (0.2)(0.1) + (0.3)(0.2) + (0.5)(0.5) = 0.02 + 0.06 + 0.25 = 0.33$$

**Final $P^2$ matrix:**
$$P^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.50 & 0.33 & 0.17 \\ 0.44 & 0.34 & 0.22 \\ 0.34 & 0.33 & 0.33 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Sanity check:** Row sums = $0.50+0.33+0.17=1.00$ ✓; $0.44+0.34+0.22=1.00$ ✓; $0.34+0.33+0.33=1.00$ ✓.

### Solution — Part (c): Steady-State Distribution

**Setup:** We need $\pi = (\pi_G, \pi_M, \pi_R)$ such that $\pi P = \pi$ and $\pi_G + \pi_M + \pi_R = 1$.

**Step 1:** Write the system $\pi P = \pi$:
$$\pi_G = 0.6 \pi_G + 0.4 \pi_M + 0.2 \pi_R$$
$$\pi_M = 0.3 \pi_G + 0.4 \pi_M + 0.3 \pi_R$$
$$\pi_R = 0.1 \pi_G + 0.2 \pi_M + 0.5 \pi_R$$

**Step 2:** Replace $\pi_R = 1 - \pi_G - \pi_M$ in the first two equations.

**Equation 1:**
$$\pi_G = 0.6 \pi_G + 0.4 \pi_M + 0.2(1 - \pi_G - \pi_M)$$
$$\pi_G = 0.6 \pi_G + 0.4 \pi_M + 0.2 - 0.2 \pi_G - 0.2 \pi_M$$
$$\pi_G = 0.4 \pi_G + 0.2 \pi_M + 0.2$$
$$0.6 \pi_G - 0.2 \pi_M = 0.2$$
$$3 \pi_G - \pi_M = 1 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Equation 2:**
$$\pi_M = 0.3 \pi_G + 0.4 \pi_M + 0.3(1 - \pi_G - \pi_M)$$
$$\pi_M = 0.3 \pi_G + 0.4 \pi_M + 0.3 - 0.3 \pi_G - 0.3 \pi_M$$
$$\pi_M = 0.1 \pi_M + 0.3$$
$$0.9 \pi_M = 0.3$$
$$\pi_M = \frac{0.3}{0.9} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 3:** From (I): $3 \pi_G - \frac{1}{3} = 1 \implies 3\pi_G = \frac{4}{3} \implies \pi_G = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444$.

**Step 4:** $\pi_R = 1 - \pi_G - \pi_M = 1 - \frac{4}{9} - \frac{3}{9} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$.

**Steady-state distribution:**
$$\pi = \left(\frac{4}{9}, \frac{3}{9}, \frac{2}{9}\right) = (0.4444, 0.3333, 0.2222)$$

**Engineering interpretation:** In the long run, the machine spends $44.44\%$ of days in *Good* condition, $33.33\%$ needing *Minor* repair, and $22.22\%$ needing *Major* repair. The workshop manager can use this to plan maintenance crew schedules.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transition Diagram for the Workshop Example

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the workshop machine's state transitions with edge labels showing the one-step probabilities from matrix $P$:

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeG["State G Good"]
    nodeM["State M Minor Repair"]
    nodeR["State R Major Repair"]
    nodeG -->|0.6| nodeG
    nodeG -->|0.3| nodeM
    nodeG -->|0.1| nodeR
    nodeM -->|0.4| nodeG
    nodeM -->|0.4| nodeM
    nodeM -->|0.2| nodeR
    nodeR -->|0.2| nodeG
    nodeR -->|0.3| nodeM
    nodeR -->|0.5| nodeR
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Markov Chain Analysis

This diagram maps the *process* of analyzing a Markov chain problem, from data input to engineering output:

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeInput["INPUT: Transition Matrix P and Initial State"]
    nodeProp["STEP 1: Validate Stochasticity rows sum to 1, entries non-negative"]
    nodeCls["STEP 2: Classify States transient, recurrent, absorbing, periodic"]
    nodeIrr["STEP 3: Check Irreducibility all states communicate"]
    nodeAper["STEP 4: Check Aperiodicity gcd of return times equals 1"]
    nodePwr["STEP 5: Compute P to the n for short-term predictions"]
    nodeStat["STEP 6: Solve pi P equals pi and sum pi equals 1 for steady-state pi"]
    nodeOut["OUTPUT: Long-run frequencies, mean recurrence times, hitting probabilities"]
    nodeInput --> nodeProp
    nodeProp --> nodeCls
    nodeCls --> nodeIrr
    nodeIrr --> nodeAper
    nodeAper --> nodePwr
    nodeAper --> nodeStat
    nodePwr --> nodeOut
    nodeStat --> nodeOut
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX0["X0 initial state"]
    nodeX1["X1 next state"]
    nodeX2["X2 next next state"]
    nodeXn["Xn n-th state"]
    nodeArrow1["Markov p X1 given X0"]
    nodeArrow2["Markov p X2 given X1"]
    nodeArrowN["Markov p Xn given Xn minus 1"]
    nodeX0 -->|nodeArrow1| nodeX1
    nodeX1 -->|nodeArrow2| nodeX2
    nodeX2 -.->|nodeArrowN| nodeXn
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree of Markov Chain States

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["State i in Markov Chain"]
    nodeQ["Can the chain leave i and possibly return infinitely often?"]
    nodeTrans["Transient positive probability of never returning"]
    nodeRec["Recurrent returns with probability 1"]
    nodeNull["Null recurrent expected return time is infinite"]
    nodePos["Positive recurrent expected return time is finite"]
    nodeAbs["Special: Absorbing state pi_ii equals 1 once entered cannot leave"]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeTrans
    nodeQ --> nodeRec
    nodeRec --> nodeNull
    nodeRec --> nodePos
    nodePos --> nodeAbs
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a Markov chain. State and explain the Markov property. Mention two real-world applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Markov chain** is a discrete-time stochastic process $\{X_n, n \geq 0\}$ defined on a countable state space $S$ such that the conditional probability of any future state depends only on the *current* state, not on the sequence of past states.

**The Markov Property (Memoryless Property):**
$$P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1} = i_{n-1}, \ldots, X_0 = i_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$$

**Explanation:** The future evolution of the process is *conditionally independent* of the past, given the present. Thus the entire history before $X_n$ carries no additional information about $X_{n+1}$ beyond what $X_n$ itself provides.

**Two Real-World Applications:**
1. **Queueing systems** — modeling customer arrivals and service completions in telecommunication and computing networks.
2. **Credit rating migrations** — Standard & Poor's / Moody's use Markov chains to model transitions between credit grades (AAA → AA → A, etc.) for portfolio risk management.
3. *(Acceptable additional examples: weather modeling, inventory replenishment, brand-switching behavior, page ranking, genetic drift.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supple Model]
**For a Markov chain with state space $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and transition matrix given below, classify each state as transient, recurrent, or absorbing. Justify.**
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify absorbing states.** A state $i$ is absorbing if $p_{ii} = 1$. Inspecting the diagonal: $p_{11} = 1$ and $p_{33} = 1$. Therefore **state 1 is absorbing** and **state 3 is absorbing**.

**Step 2 — State 2 is transient.** From state 2, the chain moves to state 1 with probability $0.3$, to state 3 with probability $0.2$, and stays at 2 with probability $0.5$. The probability of *eventually* being absorbed in either state 1 or state 3 (rather than staying at 2 forever, which has zero probability in a finite chain with absorbing states) is $1.0$. The probability of *ever returning* to state 2 after leaving is $0.5$ (only if it stays at 2 the next step), and the expected number of returns is geometric and finite. Therefore **state 2 is transient**.

**Conclusion:** States **1 and 3 are absorbing (recurrent)**, and state **2 is transient**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** A transient state is one that the chain eventually leaves and does not return to in the long run. In a quality-control inspection process, a "defective" state is often transient because items get repaired or discarded permanently.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, End Semester]

A taxi company has classified its service status daily as: *Excellent* (E), *Good* (G), and *Poor* (P). The one-step transition matrix is:
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.4 & 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** (7 marks) Compute the 2-step transition matrix $P^2$ and the 3-step transition matrix $P^3$. If the service is currently *Good*, find the probability that it will be *Poor* after 3 days.

**(b)** (7 marks) Determine the steady-state (stationary) distribution. Comment on the long-run behavior of the system.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — 2-step and 3-step transition matrices

**Computing $P^2$** (we apply the rule $p_{ij}^{(2)} = \sum_k p_{ik} p_{kj}$):

Row 1:
$$p_{11}^{(2)} = 0.5(0.5) + 0.3(0.4) + 0.2(0.2) = 0.25 + 0.12 + 0.04 = 0.41$$
$$p_{12}^{(2)} = 0.5(0.3) + 0.3(0.4) + 0.2(0.4) = 0.15 + 0.12 + 0.08 = 0.35$$
$$p_{13}^{(2)} = 0.5(0.2) + 0.3(0.2) + 0.2(0.4) = 0.10 + 0.06 + 0.08 = 0.24$$

Row 2:
$$p_{21}^{(2)} = 0.4(0.5) + 0.4(0.4) + 0.2(0.2) = 0.20 + 0.16 + 0.04 = 0.40$$
$$p_{22}^{(2)} = 0.4(0.3) + 0.4(0.4) + 0.2(0.4) = 0.12 + 0.16 + 0.08 = 0.36$$
$$p_{23}^{(2)} = 0.4(0.2) + 0.4(0.2) + 0.2(0.4) = 0.08 + 0.08 + 0.08 = 0.24$$

Row 3:
$$p_{31}^{(2)} = 0.2(0.5) + 0.4(0.4) + 0.4(0.2) = 0.10 + 0.16 + 0.08 = 0.34$$
$$p_{32}^{(2)} = 0.2(0.3) + 0.4(0.4) + 0.4(0.4) = 0.06 + 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.38$$
$$p_{33}^{(2)} = 0.2(0.2) + 0.4(0.2) + 0.4(0.4) = 0.04 + 0.08 + 0.16 = 0.28$$

**Therefore:**
$$P^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.41 & 0.35 & 0.24 \\ 0.40 & 0.36 & 0.24 \\ 0.34 & 0.38 & 0.28 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Computing $P^3 = P^2 \cdot P$:** (showing just the row we need)

For $p_{23}^{(3)}$, we use the second row of $P^2$ and the third column of $P$:
$$p_{23}^{(3)} = (0.40)(0.2) + (0.36)(0.2) + (0.24)(0.4) = 0.08 + 0.072 + 0.096 = 0.248$$

**Answer to sub-part's specific question:** The probability that starting from *Good*, the service is *Poor* after 3 days is $p_{23}^{(3)} = 0.248$, i.e., **24.8%**.

### Part (b) — Steady-State Distribution

**Set up the system $\pi P = \pi$ with $\pi_E + \pi_G + \pi_P = 1$:**

Equations:
$$\pi_E = 0.5\pi_E + 0.4\pi_G + 0.2\pi_P$$
$$\pi_G = 0.3\pi_E + 0.4\pi_G + 0.4\pi_P$$
$$\pi_P = 0.2\pi_E + 0.2\pi_G + 0.4\pi_P$$

**Rearrange using $\pi_E + \pi_G + \pi_P = 1$:**

From equation 1: $0.5\pi_E - 0.4\pi_G - 0.2\pi_P = 0$, multiply by 10: $5\pi_E - 4\pi_G - 2\pi_P = 0$.

Substitute $\pi_P = 1 - \pi_E - \pi_G$:
$$5\pi_E - 4\pi_G - 2(1 - \pi_E - \pi_G) = 0$$
$$5\pi_E - 4\pi_G - 2 + 2\pi_E + 2\pi_G = 0$$
$$7\pi_E - 2\pi_G = 2 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

From equation 2: $-0.3\pi_E + 0.6\pi_G - 0.4\pi_P = 0$, multiply by 10: $-3\pi_E + 6\pi_G - 4\pi_P = 0$.

Substitute $\pi_P = 1 - \pi_E - \pi_G$:
$$-3\pi_E + 6\pi_G - 4(1 - \pi_E - \pi_G) = 0$$
$$-3\pi_E + 6\pi_G - 4 + 4\pi_E + 4\pi_G = 0$$
$$\pi_E + 10\pi_G = 4 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Solving (I) and (II):** From (I): $\pi_E = \frac{2 + 2\pi_G}{7}$. Substitute into (II):
$$\frac{2 + 2\pi_G}{7} + 10\pi_G = 4$$
$$2 + 2\pi_G + 70\pi_G = 28$$
$$72\pi_G = 26 \implies \pi_G = \frac{26}{72} = \frac{13}{36} \approx 0.3611$$

Then $\pi_E = \frac{2 + 2(13/36)}{7} = \frac{2 + 26/36}{7} = \frac{72/36 + 26/36}{7} = \frac{98/36}{7} = \frac{98}{252} = \frac{7}{18} \approx 0.3889$.

And $\pi_P = 1 - \frac{7}{18} - \frac{13}{36} = \frac{36 - 14 - 13}{36} = \frac{9}{36} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$.

**Final steady-state distribution:**
$$\pi = \left(\frac{7}{18}, \frac{13}{36}, \frac{1}{4}\right) \approx (0.3889, 0.3611, 0.2500)$$

**Long-run behavior commentary:** In the long run, regardless of the initial service quality rating, the taxi company's service will be in *Excellent* condition about **38.89%** of the time, in *Good* condition about **36.11%** of the time, and in *Poor* condition about **25.00%** of the time. The chain is irreducible (every state can be reached from every other state) and aperiodic (gcd of return times is 1), guaranteeing unique convergence to $\pi$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to verify that the chain is *irreducible* and *aperiodic* before claiming uniqueness of the stationary distribution. If the chain were periodic (e.g., 2-cycle), the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n$ would not exist, and the stationary distribution would not be unique. Always state these conditions explicitly for full marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, End Semester]

A two-state Markov chain has the transition matrix:
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** (7 marks) Verify that this chain is irreducible and aperiodic. Compute the steady-state distribution $\pi$ using $\pi P = \pi$.

**(b)** (7 marks) If the chain starts in state 1, find the probability of being in state 1 after 2 steps, i.e., $p_{11}^{(2)}$. Use the Chapman–Kolmogorov equation. If the long-run probability of being in state 1 is 0.4, compute the mean recurrence time for state 1.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Irreducibility, Aperiodicity, and Steady-State

**Irreducibility check:** From state 1, $p_{12} = 0.3 > 0$. From state 2, $p_{21} = 0.4 > 0$. Therefore states 1 and 2 communicate. The chain is **irreducible**.

**Aperiodicity check:** $p_{11} = 0.7 > 0$, so period of state 1 divides 1, hence period is 1. Since the chain is irreducible, period of state 2 is also 1. The chain is **aperiodic**.

**Steady-state distribution:** Let $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2)$ with $\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$. The system $\pi P = \pi$ gives:
$$\pi_1 = 0.7\pi_1 + 0.4\pi_2$$
$$\pi_2 = 0.3\pi_1 + 0.6\pi_2$$

**Substituting $\pi_2 = 1 - \pi_1$:**
$$\pi_1 = 0.7\pi_1 + 0.4(1 - \pi_1)$$
$$\pi_1 = 0.7\pi_1 + 0.4 - 0.4\pi_1$$
$$\pi_1 = 0.3\pi_1 + 0.4$$
$$0.7\pi_1 = 0.4$$
$$\pi_1 = \frac{0.4}{0.7} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714$$

Therefore $\pi_2 = 1 - \frac{4}{7} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$.

**Steady-state distribution:** $\pi = (4/7, 3/7) \approx (0.5714, 0.4286)$.

### Part (b) — Two-Step Probability and Mean Recurrence Time

**Using Chapman–Kolmogorov for $p_{11}^{(2)}$:**
$$p_{11}^{(2)} = p_{11} \cdot p_{11} + p_{12} \cdot p_{21} = (0.7)(0.7) + (0.3)(0.4) = 0.49 + 0.12 = 0.61$$

**Mean recurrence time:** For a positive recurrent, irreducible, aperiodic Markov chain, the mean recurrence time of state $j$ is given by the elegant formula:
$$m_{jj} = \frac{1}{\pi_j}$$

Given that the long-run probability of being in state 1 is $\pi_1 = 0.4$ (as stated in the question):
$$m_{11} = \frac{1}{\pi_1} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5 \text{ steps}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** On average, the chain returns to state 1 once every 2.5 time steps in the long run.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked to *compute* the mean recurrence time, students sometimes forget the formula $m_{ii} = 1/\pi_i$ and try to compute it by simulation or by enumerating return paths. The closed-form shortcut above is universally accepted. Also note: this formula holds only for *positive recurrent* states — verify this condition is satisfied (in a finite irreducible chain it always is).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Markov Chains**

- [x] A **discrete-time stochastic process** is indexed by $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$; values are random.
- [x] The **Markov property** asserts future $\perp\!\!\!\perp$ past $\mid$ present: $P(X_{n+1} \mid X_n, X_{n-1}, \ldots) = P(X_{n+1} \mid X_n)$.
- [x] A **transition matrix** $P$ is row-stochastic: $p_{ij} \geq 0$, $\sum_j p_{ij} = 1$.
- [x] **Chapman–Kolmogorov:** $P^{(n+m)} = P^{(n)} P^{(m)}$, hence $P^{(n)} = P^n$.
- [x] **$n$-step probability** $p_{ij}^{(n)} = (P^n)_{ij}$ — direct matrix power.
- [x] **State classification:** transient (leaves forever with positive probability), recurrent (returns with prob 1), absorbing ($p_{ii} = 1$).
- [x] **Irreducible chain** = all states communicate. **Aperiodic** = period is 1.
- [x] **Steady-state distribution** $\pi$ satisfies $\pi P = \pi$ and $\sum \pi_j = 1$.
- [x] **Existence & Uniqueness:** Finite + irreducible + aperiodic $\Rightarrow$ unique $\pi$ with $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$ for all $i$.
- [x] **Mean recurrence time:** $m_{ii} = 1/\pi_i$ for positive-recurrent states.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** queueing (M/M/1), inventory, reliability, page ranking, credit risk, weather forecasting, genetics (Wright–Fisher).
- [x] **Watch out for:** periodic chains (no unique $\pi$), reducible chains (multiple $\pi$ candidates), and confusing row vs column stochasticity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When solving a 14-mark question, the typical valuation pattern is: 2 marks for setting up the system correctly, 3 marks for algebraic manipulation, and 2 marks for the final answer + interpretation. Always *interpret* the steady-state probabilities in the context of the engineering problem — a 1-mark "comment" or "significance" line is the difference between a 13 and a 14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous-Time Stochastic Processes: Poisson Processes and Continuous-Time Markov Chains - Basic Properties and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT867 - STOCHASTIC DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous-Time Stochastic Processes: Poisson Processes and Continuous-Time Markov Chains - Basic Properties and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Continuous-Time Stochastic Processes — Formal Definition

A **stochastic process** $\{X(t) : t \in T\}$ is a family of random variables indexed by a continuous parameter $t \in T \subseteq [0,\infty)$. For KTU's PECAT867 syllabus, we focus on two flagship continuous-time processes:

1. **The Poisson Process** $N(t)$ — a *counting* process.
2. **Continuous-Time Markov Chains (CTMC)** $X(t)$ — a *state-space* process taking values in a discrete set $S = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (PECAT867 / Module 1):** *Define continuous-time stochastic processes; the Poisson process; basic properties of the Poisson process; continuous-time Markov chains; limiting/stationary distributions; birth-death processes; applications.*

### 1.1.1 The Poisson Process — Definition

A counting process $\{N(t), t \ge 0\}$ is a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$** if:

$$
P\bigl(N(t) = n\bigr) = \frac{e^{-\lambda t} (\lambda t)^{n}}{n!}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$

The process satisfies three axioms (Karlin & Taylor convention used in KTU):
- **(A1)** $N(0) = 0$ with probability $1$.
- **(A2)** *Independent increments:* the number of events in disjoint time intervals are independent.
- **(A3)** *Stationary increments:* the distribution of $N(t+s) - N(s)$ depends only on $t$.
- **(A4)** $P(N(h) = 1) = \lambda h + o(h)$ and $P(N(h) \ge 2) = o(h)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:** The rate $\lambda$ has units of **events per unit time** (e.g., arrivals/sec, failures/hr). The mean inter-arrival time is $1/\lambda$ time units.

### 1.1.2 Continuous-Time Markov Chain — Definition

A stochastic process $\{X(t), t \ge 0\}$ on a discrete state space $S$ is a **CTMC** if for every $n$ and all time-points $0 \le t_1 < t_2 < \dots < t_n < t < s$:

$$
P\bigl(X(s) = j \mid X(t) = i, X(t_n) = i_n, \dots, X(t_1) = i_1\bigr) = P\bigl(X(s) = j \mid X(t) = i\bigr)
$$

In words: **the future is independent of the past given the present** — the continuous-time analog of the Markov property.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Shop Counter":**
>
> Imagine a small bakery counter. Customers walk in at random times. Let $N(t)$ count the number of customers served by time $t$. The *Poisson process* is the perfect model: customers arrive "one at a time," the rate $\lambda$ doesn't change during the day (stationarity), and arrivals in the morning are independent of those in the evening (independence).
>
> Now, the **CTMC** is like a *machine* that cycles through states — *Idle → Heating → Working → Cooling → Idle* — and the *time spent in each state* is exponentially distributed. Because the exponential is *memoryless*, the moment the machine leaves one state, the past is forgotten; only the current state governs the next transition.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of CTMC Behaviour

| Pillar | Symbol | Intuition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Generator (Q-matrix)** | $Q = [q_{ij}]$ | Instantaneous "jump" rates between states. |
| **Transition function** | $P(t) = [P_{ij}(t)]$ | Probability of being in state $j$ after time $t$, starting from $i$. |
| **Limiting distribution** | $\pi$ | Long-run fraction of time the chain spends in each state. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Every KTU question on this module ultimately tests one of the three pillars above. Memorize the *Q-matrix row-sum property* and the *limiting-distribution equation* $\pi Q = 0$ — they appear in **nearly 70%** of past university-exam problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Poisson Process — Core Properties

### 2.1.1 Inter-Arrival Times

Let $T_1, T_2, \dots$ be the successive inter-arrival times of a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$. Then:

$$
T_k \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda), \quad \text{independent}, \quad f_{T_k}(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t},\ t \ge 0
$$

The arrival time of the $n$-th event is $S_n = T_1 + T_2 + \cdots + T_n$, with:

$$
S_n \sim \text{Gamma}(n, \lambda), \quad f_{S_n}(t) = \frac{\lambda^{n} t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}
$$

### 2.1.2 Memoryless Property

$$
P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The memoryless property is what links the Poisson process to CTMC. The *time spent in a state* of a CTMC is exponentially distributed precisely because the exponential is the *only* continuous memoryless distribution.

### 2.1.3 Decomposition & Superposition

- **Decomposition:** If each event of a Poisson($\lambda$) process is independently classified as Type-1 with probability $p$ and Type-2 with probability $1-p$, then the two sub-processes are **independent Poisson** processes with rates $\lambda p$ and $\lambda(1-p)$.
- **Superposition:** Sum of independent Poisson($\lambda_1$) and Poisson($\lambda_2$) processes is Poisson($\lambda_1 + \lambda_2$).

## 2.2 CTMC — Generator Matrix $Q$

The Q-matrix (or **infinitesimal generator**) of a CTMC is defined entry-wise:

$$
q_{ij} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P_{ij}(h) - \delta_{ij}}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(X(t+h)=j \mid X(t)=i)}{h}, \quad i \neq j
$$

$$
q_{ii} = -\sum_{j \neq i} q_{ij} = -\lambda_i
$$

The diagonal entry $q_{ii} = -\lambda_i$ is the **negative of the total exit rate** from state $i$. The expected holding time in state $i$ is $1/\lambda_i$ (when $\lambda_i > 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row-Sum Rule (mandatory check):** Every row of $Q$ must sum to **zero**. If your computed $Q$ violates this, the entire answer is invalidated. This is the *single most common* KTU exam deduction.

## 2.3 Kolmogorov's Differential Equations

The transition matrix $P(t)$ satisfies the **forward** (Fokker–Planck for CTMC) and **backward** equations:

$$
\frac{dP(t)}{dt} = P(t) Q \quad \text{(Forward)} \qquad \frac{dP(t)}{dt} = Q P(t) \quad \text{(Backward)}
$$

with the initial condition $P(0) = I$ (identity matrix). The unique solution is:

$$
P(t) = e^{Qt} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(Qt)^{n}}{n!}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Don't confuse Forward and Backward.** When finding **state probabilities** $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}(0) P(t)$, use the **forward** equation. When finding **expected sojourn times** or **first-passage** quantities, use the **backward** equation. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.

## 2.4 Limiting / Stationary Distribution

If a CTMC is **irreducible** and **positive recurrent**, then $\lim_{t \to \infty} P(X(t) = j) = \pi_j$ exists and is independent of the initial state. The vector $\pi = (\pi_0, \pi_1, \dots)$ satisfies:

$$
\pi Q = 0, \qquad \sum_{j \in S} \pi_j = 1, \qquad \pi_j \ge 0
$$

A *limiting* distribution equals a *stationary* distribution when it exists and is unique.

## 2.5 Birth–Death Processes — The KTU Favourite

A CTMC on $S = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ with **birth rate** $\lambda_n$ from state $n$ and **death rate** $\mu_n$ from state $n$ (with $\mu_0 = 0$). The Q-matrix is tridiagonal with:

$$
q_{n, n+1} = \lambda_n, \quad q_{n, n-1} = \mu_n, \quad q_{n,n} = -(\lambda_n + \mu_n)
$$

**Limiting distribution** (when $\lambda_n, \mu_n > 0$ for $n \ge 1$):

$$
\pi_0 = \left( 1 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda_{k-1}}{\mu_k} \right)^{-1}, \qquad \pi_n = \pi_0 \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda_{k-1}}{\mu_k}
$$

For the simplest **M/M/1 birth–death** case with constant rates $\lambda_n = \lambda$ and $\mu_n = \mu$:

$$
\pi_n = \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right)^{n}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$

provided $\lambda < \mu$ (ergodicity / traffic-intensity condition).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Note |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Poisson pmf | $P(N(t)=n) = e^{-\lambda t} (\lambda t)^{n}/n!$ | $n \in \{0,1,2,\dots\}$ |
| 2 | Inter-arrival density | $f_T(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential$(\lambda)$ |
| 3 | Mean / Variance of $N(t)$ | $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$, $\text{Var}(N(t)) = \lambda t$ | Equal mean and variance |
| 4 | Sum of independent Poissons | Poisson$(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots)$ | Superposition |
| 5 | Memoryless property | $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential only |
| 6 | CTMC transition rate | $q_{ij} = \lim_{h\to 0} P_{ij}(h)/h$ | $i \neq j$ |
| 7 | Row sum of $Q$ | $\sum_{j} q_{ij} = 0$ | Mandatory check |
| 8 | Holding time in state $i$ | $E[\text{time in } i] = 1/\lambda_i$ | $\lambda_i = -q_{ii}$ |
| 9 | Kolmogorov forward | $P'(t) = P(t) Q$ | Initial $P(0)=I$ |
| 10 | Kolmogorov backward | $P'(t) = Q P(t)$ | For first-passage quantities |
| 11 | Limiting distribution | $\pi Q = 0$, $\sum \pi_j = 1$ | Irreducible, positive recurrent |
| 12 | Birth–Death $\pi_n$ | $\pi_n = \pi_0 \prod_{k=1}^{n} (\lambda_{k-1}/\mu_k)$ | $\mu_0 = 0$ |
| 13 | M/M/1 stationary | $\pi_n = (1-\rho)\rho^{n}$ | $\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these processes are used in industry (relevant for viva & project presentations):**
>
> - **Telecommunications:** Modelling call arrivals (Poisson) and handoffs between base stations (CTMC on cell IDs).
> - **Manufacturing & Reliability:** Failure times of components → exponential inter-failure times; multi-stage systems → CTMC on operating states.
> - **Computer Networks:** Packet arrivals in routers, M/M/1 queue modelling.
> - **Biology & Epidemiology:** Birth–death models for population dynamics; SIR models as CTMC.
> - **Finance:** High-frequency trade arrivals as Poisson; regime-switching asset prices as CTMC.
> - **IoT & Smart Grids:** Sensor event streams (Poisson); device state cycles (CTMC).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Poisson pmf from the Three Axioms

**Goal:** Show that the three axioms (A1–A4) force $P(N(t)=n) = e^{-\lambda t} (\lambda t)^n / n!$.

**Step 1 — Base case $n=0$.** Let $P_0(t) = P(N(t) = 0)$. Conditioning on the first instant $h$:

$$
P_0(t + h) = P_0(t) \cdot P(\text{no arrival in } (t, t+h]) = P_0(t) \cdot \bigl(1 - \lambda h + o(h)\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Form the differential equation.** Rearrange:

$$
P_0(t + h) - P_0(t) = -\lambda h P_0(t) + o(h)
$$

Divide by $h$ and let $h \to 0$:

$$
\frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the ODE** with initial condition $P_0(0) = 1$:

$$
P_0(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — Inductive step for general $n$.** Let $P_n(t) = P(N(t)=n)$. Condition on $N(t+h)=n$; events in $(0,t]$ contribute $k$ and $(t,t+h]$ contribute $n-k$:

$$
P_n(t + h) = P_n(t)\,(1 - \lambda h) + P_{n-1}(t)\, \lambda h + o(h), \quad n \ge 1
$$

The $o(h)$ term absorbs the probability of $\ge 2$ events in $(t,t+h]$.

**Step 5 — Take the limit $h \to 0$:**

$$
\frac{dP_n}{dt} = -\lambda P_n(t) + \lambda P_{n-1}(t)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve by induction.** Guess the form $P_n(t) = e^{-\lambda t} (\lambda t)^n / n!$. Verify:

- $n=0$: $P_0(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ ✓ (matches Step 3).
- Assume true for $n-1$. Compute derivative:

$$
\frac{dP_n}{dt} = -\lambda e^{-\lambda t} \frac{(\lambda t)^n}{n!} + e^{-\lambda t} \frac{n \lambda^{n} t^{n-1}}{n!} = -\lambda P_n + \lambda P_{n-1} \quad \checkmark
$$

Hence the closed form is established.

$$
\boxed{P(N(t) = n) = \frac{e^{-\lambda t} (\lambda t)^{n}}{n!}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Limiting Distribution of M/M/1 Birth–Death Chain

**Setup:** States $\{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$, birth rate $\lambda_n = \lambda$, death rate $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n \ge 1$, $\mu_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Balance equations.** A state $n$ is reached from $n-1$ (birth) and left to $n+1$ (birth) or to $n-1$ (death). In equilibrium, *flow-in = flow-out*:

$$
(\lambda + \mu)\,\pi_n = \lambda\,\pi_{n-1} + \mu\,\pi_{n+1}, \quad n \ge 1
$$

For $n = 0$:

$$
\lambda\,\pi_0 = \mu\,\pi_1
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the recurrence.** From $n=0$ equation: $\pi_1 = (\lambda/\mu) \pi_0$.

From the $n=1$ balance: $(\lambda + \mu)\pi_1 = \lambda \pi_0 + \mu \pi_2$. Substituting $\pi_1$:

$$
\mu \pi_2 = (\lambda + \mu) \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \pi_0 - \lambda \pi_0 = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{\mu} \pi_0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \pi_2 = \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right)^{2} \pi_0
$$

**Step 3 — Inductive generalisation.** Assume $\pi_n = \rho^{n} \pi_0$ with $\rho = \lambda/\mu$. Plug into the balance equation:

$$
(\lambda + \mu) \rho^{n} \pi_0 = \lambda \rho^{n-1} \pi_0 + \mu \rho^{n+1} \pi_0
$$

Divide by $\pi_0$ and factor $\rho^{n-1}$:

$$
(\lambda + \mu)\rho = \lambda + \mu \rho^{2} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \mu \rho^{2} - (\lambda + \mu)\rho + \lambda = 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; (\rho - 1)(\mu\rho - \lambda) = 0
$$

Roots: $\rho = 1$ or $\rho = \lambda/\mu$. For $\rho < 1$ the distribution is **normalizable**; we keep $\rho = \lambda/\mu$.

**Step 4 — Normalize.** Use $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \pi_n = 1$:

$$
\pi_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^{n} = \pi_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho} = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \pi_0 = 1 - \rho
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\pi_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^{n}, \quad \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Mini-Examples

### 3.3.1 Example A — Poisson Probability

> A call centre receives calls at rate $\lambda = 3$/min. Find the probability of receiving exactly 5 calls in 2 minutes, and the mean number of calls in 10 minutes.

**Solution:**

$$
P(N(2) = 5) = \frac{e^{-3 \cdot 2} (3 \cdot 2)^{5}}{5!} = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 6^{5}}{120} = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 7776}{120} = 64.8\, e^{-6} \approx 0.1606
$$

Mean in 10 minutes: $E[N(10)] = \lambda \cdot 10 = 30$ calls.

### 3.3.2 Example B — CTMC Limiting Distribution (3-State System)

> A machine has states {0 = Idle, 1 = Working, 2 = Repair} with generator:
>
> $$
> Q = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & -5 & 3 \\ 0 & 4 & -4 \end{pmatrix}
> $$

**Step 1 — Write $\pi Q = 0$:**

$$
-\pi_0 + 2\pi_1 = 0 \quad (1)
$$
$$
\pi_0 - 5\pi_1 + 4\pi_2 = 0 \quad (2)
$$
$$
3\pi_1 - 4\pi_2 = 0 \quad (3)
$$

**Step 2 — From (1):** $\pi_0 = 2\pi_1$. **From (3):** $\pi_2 = (3/4) \pi_1$.

**Step 3 — Normalize:**

$$
2\pi_1 + \pi_1 + \frac{3}{4}\pi_1 = \pi_1 \left(\frac{15}{4}\right) = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \pi_1 = \frac{4}{15}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\pi_0 = \frac{8}{15}, \quad \pi_1 = \frac{4}{15}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{3}{15} = \frac{1}{5}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by sanity check:** Sum $= 8/15 + 4/15 + 3/15 = 15/15 = 1$ ✓. The chain spends the most time *Idle* (highest $\pi$) because its exit rate is the smallest ($q_{00} = -1$).

### 3.3.3 Example C — First-Passage via Kolmogorov Backward

> For a two-state CTMC with $Q = \begin{pmatrix} -\mu & \mu \\ \lambda & -\lambda \end{pmatrix}$, find $P_{00}(t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the backward equation** for the $(0,0)$ entry:

$$
\frac{dP_{00}}{dt} = -\mu P_{00}(t) + \mu P_{10}(t)
$$

We need a second equation. By the forward equation, $P_{10}(t) = 1 - P_{00}(t)$ (since $P_{00}(t) + P_{10}(t) = 1$ in a 2-state chain).

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\frac{dP_{00}}{dt} = -\mu P_{00}(t) + \mu \bigl(1 - P_{00}(t)\bigr) = \mu - 2\mu P_{00}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear ODE** with $P_{00}(0) = 1$. Steady state: $P_{00}^{*} = 1/2$. Solution:

$$
P_{00}(t) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2\mu t} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu) t}
$$
