## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Define a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). List its components and write the belief update equation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A POMDP is the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, T, Z, R, \gamma)$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is the state space, $\mathcal{A}$ the action space, $\mathcal{O}$ the observation space, $T(s,a,s')$ the transition probability, $Z(o,s')$ the observation probability, $R(s,a)$ the reward, and $\gamma \in [0,1)$ the discount factor. **(1 mark)**
> Because the true state is unobserved, the agent maintains a **belief** $b(s)$, a probability distribution over $\mathcal{S}$. **(1 mark)**
> Belief update after action $a$ and observation $o$:
>
> $$b'(s') = \eta\, Z(o, s') \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} T(s, a, s')\, b(s)$$
>
> where $\eta$ is a normalizer ensuring $\sum_{s'} b'(s') = 1$. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q2.** What is the Robbins-Monro algorithm? State the two conditions on the step-size sequence $\{\alpha_k\}$ for almost-sure convergence.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Robbins-Monro algorithm is a recursive stochastic procedure to find the root $\theta^*$ of a function $h(\theta) = E[Y \mid \theta] = 0$ using only noisy observations $Y_k$. **(1 mark)**
> The update rule is:
>
> $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \alpha_k Y_k$$
>
> **(1 mark)**
> The two Robbins-Monro step-size conditions for almost-sure convergence are:
>
> $$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k = \infty, \qquad \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k^2 < \infty$$
>
> **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Kalman filter as a state estimator for a linear-Gaussian system. Derive the Kalman gain expression from the principle of minimum posterior variance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An autonomous vehicle uses a 1-D Kalman filter to estimate its position. The state is $x_t = [p_t,\, v_t]^\top$ where $p_t$ is position and $v_t$ is velocity. The dynamics are:
> $$x_{t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} x_t + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u_t + w_t,\quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, Q),\ Q = 0.01 I$$
> The position-only measurement model is $z_t = [1\ 0]\, x_t + v_t$ with $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, R),\ R = 0.1$. Given $\hat{x}_{0\mid 0} = [0,\, 0]^\top$ and $P_{0\mid 0} = I_2$, compute the predicted state and updated state for one cycle with $\Delta t = 1$, $u_0 = 0.5$, and measurement $z_1 = 1.2$. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution (a) — Kalman Filter Derivation (7 marks):**
>
> The Kalman filter operates as a recursive **predict-update** cycle on the belief (state distribution). **(1 mark)**
>
> **System model (assumed):**
> $$x_t = A x_{t-1} + B u_t + w_t,\quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$
> $$z_t = H x_t + v_t,\quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$
>
> **Predict step:** the prior at time $t$ given all measurements up to $t-1$ is:
> $$\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = A \hat{x}_{t-1 \mid t-1} + B u_t$$
> $$P_{t \mid t-1} = A P_{t-1 \mid t-1} A^\top + Q$$
> **(1 mark for predict mean, 1 mark for predict covariance)**
>
> **Update step:** Upon receiving $z_t$, the posterior is computed using the Bayes rule for Gaussians. The innovation is $\nu_t = z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}$. The Kalman gain is defined as:
> $$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H^\top (H P_{t \mid t-1} H^\top + R)^{-1}$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Derivation of $K_t$ by minimum-variance principle:** The posterior covariance is
> $$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t \mid t-1}$$
> To minimize the trace (sum of posterior variances) w.r.t. $K_t$:
> $$\frac{\partial}{\partial K_t} \mathrm{tr}(P_{t \mid t}) = -2 (H P_{t \mid t-1})^\top + 2 K_t H P_{t \mid t-1} H^\top = 0$$
> Solving:
> $$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H^\top (H P_{t \mid t-1} H^\top + R)^{-1}$$
> **(2 marks for the derivation)**
>
> **Final update:**
> $$\hat{x}_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t, \qquad P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t \mid t-1}$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Interpretation:** When $R$ (measurement noise) is small, $K_t$ is large and the filter trusts $z_t$. When $Q$ (process noise) is small, $K_t$ is small and the filter trusts the prediction.
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution (b) — Numerical Cycle (7 marks):**
>
> **Given:** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $H = [1\ 0]$, $Q = 0.01 I_2$, $R = 0.1$, $\hat{x}_{0 \mid 0} = [0, 0]^\top$, $P_{0 \mid 0} = I_2$, $u_0 = 0.5$, $z_1 = 1.2$.
>
> **Step 1 — Predict mean:**
> $$\hat{x}_{1 \mid 0} = A \hat{x}_{0 \mid 0} + B u_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}(0.5) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 2 — Predict covariance:**
> $$P_{1 \mid 0} = A P_{0 \mid 0} A^\top + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}$$
> **(2 marks)**
>
> **Step 3 — Kalman gain:**
> $$H P_{1 \mid 0} = [1\ 0] \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} = [2.01\ \ 1]$$
> $$H P_{1 \mid 0} H^\top = 2.01$$
> $$H P_{1 \mid 0} H^\top + R = 2.01 + 0.1 = 2.11$$
> $$K_1 = \frac{1}{2.11} \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9526 \\ 0.4739 \end{bmatrix}$$
> **(2 marks)**
>
> **Step 4 — Innovation and update:**
> $$\nu_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1 \mid 0} = 1.2 - 0 = 1.2$$
> $$\hat{x}_{1 \mid 1} = \hat{x}_{1 \mid 0} + K_1 \nu_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + 1.2 \begin{bmatrix} 0.9526 \\ 0.4739 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1431 \\ 1.0687 \end{bmatrix}$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 5 — Posterior covariance (optional credit):**
> $$P_{1 \mid 1} = (I - K_1 H) P_{1 \mid 0} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.9526 & 0 \\ -0.4739 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0953 & 0.0474 \\ 0.0474 & 0.5361 \end{bmatrix}$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Final answer:** $\hat{x}_{1 \mid 1} = [1.143,\ 1.069]^\top$, $P_{1 \mid 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.095 & 0.047 \\ 0.047 & 0.536 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

> **ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the use of Hidden Markov Models in Part-of-Speech tagging. Write the Viterbi algorithm and explain each step. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the HMM for a toy two-word sentence with tags {N, V} (Noun, Verb) and observations $\{w_1, w_2\}$. The parameters are:
>
> $$\pi = [0.6,\ 0.4], \quad A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> where $B_{ij} = P(w_i \mid \text{tag}_j)$ and the rows of $A$ are $P(\text{tag}_t \mid \text{tag}_{t-1})$ in the order [N, V]. Find the most probable tag sequence using the Viterbi algorithm. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution (a) — HMM for POS Tagging (7 marks):**
>
> POS tagging assigns a grammatical category (noun, verb, adjective, …) to every word in a sentence. **(1 mark)**
> The same word can belong to multiple categories (e.g., "book" = noun or verb), creating **lexical ambiguity**. A deterministic rule-based tagger cannot resolve this; a probabilistic HMM weighs the contextual evidence. **(1 mark)**
>
> The HMM assumes the true tag sequence is a **Markov chain** of order 1, and each word is conditionally independent of all other words and tags given its own tag. The joint probability of an observed sentence $W = (w_1, \dots, w_T)$ and tag sequence $S = (s_1, \dots, s_T)$ is:
>
> $$P(W, S) = \pi_{s_1} \prod_{t=1}^{T} b_{s_t}(w_t) \cdot \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{s_{t-1}, s_t}$$
>
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Viterbi Algorithm — step by step:**
>
> **Initialization ($t = 1$):** $\delta_1(j) = \pi_j b_j(w_1)$, $\psi_1(j) = 0$.
> **Recursion ($t = 2, \dots, T$):** $\delta_t(j) = \max_i [\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{i,j}] b_j(w_t)$, $\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_i [\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{i,j}]$.
> **Termination:** $P^* = \max_j \delta_T(j)$, $s_T^* = \arg\max_j \delta_T(j)$.
> **Backtrace:** $s_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(s_{t+1}^*)$ for $t = T-1, \dots, 1$.
> **(3 marks)**
>
> **Complexity:** $O(T \cdot N^2)$ for $N$ tags. **Use:** Viterbi yields the single best tag sequence, in contrast to the **Forward-Backward** algorithm which computes the posterior $P(s_t \mid W)$ at every position.
> **(1 mark)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution (b) — Numerical Viterbi (7 marks):**
>
> Given $\pi = [0.6\ (N),\ 0.4\ (V)]$, transition matrix $A$ (rows: from-state, columns: to-state):
>
> $$A = \begin{bmatrix} P(N \mid N) & P(V \mid N) \\ P(N \mid V) & P(V \mid V) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Emission matrix $B$ (columns: tag, rows: word):
>
> $$B = \begin{bmatrix} P(w_1 \mid N) & P(w_1 \mid V) \\ P(w_2 \mid N) & P(w_2 \mid V) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 1 — Initialization (work in log-domain for safety, but we will use direct values):**
> $$\delta_1(N) = \pi_N b_N(w_1) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$$
> $$\delta_1(V) = \pi_V b_V(w_1) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 2 — Recursion at $t = 2$, for tag = N:**
> $$\delta_2(N) = \max[\delta_1(N) a_{N,N},\ \delta_1(V) a_{V,N}] \cdot b_N(w_2)$$
> $$= \max[0.54 \times 0.3,\ 0.08 \times 0.8] \times 0.1$$
> $$= \max[0.162,\ 0.064] \times 0.1 = 0.162 \times 0.1 = 0.0162$$
> Backpointer: $\psi_2(N) = N$. **(2 marks)**
>
> **Step 3 — Recursion at $t = 2$, for tag = V:**
> $$\delta_2(V) = \max[\delta_1(N) a_{N,V},\ \delta_1(V) a_{V,V}] \cdot b_V(w_2)$$
> $$= \max[0.54 \times 0.7,\ 0.08 \times 0.2] \times 0.8$$
> $$= \max[0.378,\ 0.016] \times 0.8 = 0.378 \times 0.8 = 0.3024$$
> Backpointer: $\psi_2(V) = N$. **(2 marks)**
>
> **Step 4 — Termination:**
> $$P^* = \max[\delta_2(N),\ \delta_2(V)] = \max[0.0162,\ 0.3024] = 0.3024$$
> $$s_2^* = \arg\max = V$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 5 — Backtrace:**
> $$s_1^* = \psi_2(s_2^*) = \psi_2(V) = N$$
>
> **Final answer:** Most probable tag sequence is $\hat{S} = (N, V)$ with probability $P^* = 0.3024$. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Interpretation:** Sentence is "Noun Verb" — the HMM has correctly identified the most plausible grammatical structure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **POMDP belief update:** Students frequently forget the **normalizer $\eta$** and present an unnormalized distribution. This costs **1 full mark**. Always show $\eta = \left[ \sum_{s'} Z(o,s') \sum_s T(s,a,s') b(s) \right]^{-1}$.
>
> 2. **Kalman gain derivation:** Writing $K_t = P H^\top (H P H^\top + R)^{-1}$ *without* deriving it from $\partial \mathrm{tr}(P)/\partial K = 0$ loses **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions. Always show the trace minimization step.
>
> 3. **Robbins-Monro:** Students often list only **one** of the two step-size conditions. The full credit requires both $\sum \alpha_k = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_k^2 < \infty$. The first ensures the algorithm can reach the root; the second ensures noise is summable.
>
> 4. **Viterbi:** A common error is using $\sum$ (sum) instead of $\max$ in the recursion, which gives the Forward algorithm (likelihood), not Viterbi (best path). Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
>
> 5. **Numerical Kalman problems:** Forgetting that $H$ is a **row vector** (not a matrix) in 1-D measurement cases and using $H^\top H$ instead of $H H^\top$ leads to a **scalar inversion error** that propagates through the whole computation.
>
> 6. **RRT vs PRM:** Students often confuse the two. **RRT** is incremental and tree-structured; **PRM** is a multi-query graph. RRT explores the frontier; PRM samples and then queries.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stochastic AI** = decisions under probabilistic uncertainty in state, observation, or action outcome; deterministic AI is the special case.
- **NLP — HMM** for POS tagging: triple $(\pi, A, B)$; joint = $\pi_{s_1} \prod b_{s_t}(w_t) \prod a_{s_{t-1}, s_t}$.
- **Viterbi algorithm**: $\max$ over previous tags (not $\sum$); dynamic programming; $O(T N^2)$; backpointers for traceback.
- **Forward-Backward** algorithm: $\sum$ over previous tags; gives per-position posteriors.
- **Naive Bayes classifier**: $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$; features assumed i.i.d. given class.
- **POMDP tuple**: $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{O}, T, Z, R, \gamma)$; belief $b$ replaces state; **belief MDP** has $\mathcal{B} = \Delta(\mathcal{S})$.
- **Belief update**: $b'(s') = \eta Z(o, s') \sum_s T(s,a,s') b(s)$.
- **Bellman optimality (MDP)**: $V^*(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s,a,s') V^*(s')]$.
- **Kalman filter** for linear-Gaussian: predict $(A, B, Q)$, update with gain $K_t = P H^\top (H P H^\top + R)^{-1}$; gain is minimum-variance optimal.
- **Path planning — PRM**: sample $N$ configurations, connect neighbors, graph search; **probabilistically complete**.
- **Path planning — RRT**: incremental tree, Voronoi-biased sampling; **probabilistically complete**, not optimal.
- **Adaptive control — Robbins-Monro**: $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \alpha_k Y_k$; conditions $\sum \alpha_k = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_k^2 < \infty$ ensure a.s. convergence.
- **Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)**: recursive least-squares estimate + certainty-equivalent control re-design.
- **Q-learning (RL)**: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$; off-policy; Robbins-Monro on Bellman error.
- **$\varepsilon$-greedy exploration**: pick $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ w.p. $1-\varepsilon$, random action w.p. $\varepsilon$; decays over training.
- **Discount factor $\gamma$**: typically 0.95 to 0.99; controls how far-sighted the agent is.
- **SLAM** = simultaneous localization and mapping; EKF-SLAM is the classic stochastic solution.
- **Sensor fusion** = combining multiple noisy sensors via Bayesian update (Kalman for linear-Gaussian, particle filter for general nonlinear).
- **Sutton & Barto** conventions: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$; on-policy = SARSA, off-policy = Q-learning.
- **Almost-sure convergence (a.s.)** = with probability 1; the strongest form of stochastic convergence.
- **Probabilistic completeness** = $\lim_{N \to \infty} P(\text{solution found}) = 1$; weaker than asymptotic optimality.
- **KTU exam tip**: Always write the **complete tuple** when defining MDP/POMDP/HMM; examiners award 1 mark for the definition alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Finance and Operations Research: Financial Modeling (Risk Management, Portfolio Optimization) and Operations Research Applications (Inventory Management, Queuing Systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT867 - STOCHASTIC DECISION MAKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications in Finance and Operations Research: Financial Modeling (Risk Management, Portfolio Optimization) and Operations Research Applications (Inventory Management, Queuing Systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — APPLICATIONS IN FINANCE AND OPERATIONS RESEARCH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stochastic Decision Making** in engineering management refers to a class of decision-theoretic frameworks in which a decision-maker (DM) selects an optimal action from a feasible set under conditions of **partial information, randomness, and probabilistic uncertainty**. When applied to the allied domains of **Financial Engineering** and **Operations Research (OR)**, it transforms the abstract axioms of probability, stochastic processes, and dynamic programming into actionable managerial policies.

In the **financial domain**, the decision-maker is an investor, risk officer, or quant analyst who must allocate capital across assets whose future returns are random variables. The two flagship applications are:

- **Risk Management** — quantifying, hedging, and controlling exposure to stochastic losses using measures such as *Value-at-Risk (VaR)*, *Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR)*, and *Sharpe Ratio*.
- **Portfolio Optimization** — selecting the asset weights that maximize expected utility (typically mean return) for a given level of risk (variance), classically formalized by **Markowitz Mean-Variance Analysis (1952)**.

In the **operations research domain**, the decision-maker is a supply-chain manager, plant operator, or service engineer who must balance stochastic demand, service times, and replenishment lead times. The two flagship applications are:

- **Inventory Management** — determining the optimal reorder quantity (*EOQ*), reorder point (*ROP*), and safety stock under stochastic demand using the **Newsvendor Model** and the **(s, S) policy**.
- **Queuing Systems** — analyzing waiting-line phenomena described by **Kendall's Notation**, characterized by birth–death processes and performance metrics such as *L*, *Lq*, *W*, *Wq*, *ρ*, and *Pn*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PECAT867 / Module 4**
> The module explicitly tests a learner's ability to **map** real-world financial and operational problems to a stochastic model, **derive** the optimal policy analytically, and **interpret** the solution in managerial terms. Marks are awarded for model formulation, not merely formula substitution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chefs at a busy restaurant kitchen** 🍳. You don't know exactly how many customers will walk in tonight (stochastic demand), how long each dish will take to cook (random service time), or how much the price of tomatoes will fluctuate tomorrow (stochastic prices).

- **Portfolio Optimization** is like deciding the *menu mix* (risky-tasty dishes vs. safe-boring dishes) to maximize profit while keeping customer complaints (risk) low.
- **Risk Management** is like buying *insurance* against food-poisoning lawsuits — you pay a small certain cost to avoid a rare but catastrophic loss.
- **Inventory Management** is like deciding *how many tomatoes to stock* each morning — too few and you run out, too many and they rot.
- **Queuing Systems** is like deciding *how many chefs* to hire and *how many tills* to open to keep the queue of hungry customers short.

In every case, randomness is the enemy of efficiency, and **stochastic decision-making is the science of making the best possible choice in spite of that randomness.**

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> The following dimensionless and dimensional metrics are universally used in KTU examination questions on this module:

- **Service Utilization (ρ)** — $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$, where $\lambda$ is the arrival rate and $\mu$ is the service rate. The system is stable **iff** $\rho < 1$.
- **Mean (μ) and Variance (σ²)** of demand distribution.
- **Risk-free rate (rf)** — typically the 10-year government bond yield, used as the benchmark in Sharpe Ratio.
- **Lead time (L)** — the time between placing and receiving a replenishment order.
- **Service level (β)** — probability of not stocking out during lead time, often 0.95 or 0.99.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Portfolio Optimization — Markowitz Mean-Variance Framework

**Theoretical Logic (Why and How):**

1. **Why?** Investors dislike risk but love return. A single asset's future return $R_i$ is a random variable. Combining assets whose returns are *not perfectly correlated* reduces the portfolio's overall variance without sacrificing expected return — the **diversification effect**.
2. **How?** Define expected return vector $\mu = E[R]$, covariance matrix $\Sigma$, and weight vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^n$ such that $\mathbf{1}^\top w = 1$ (self-financing). Solve:
   - **Minimize** $w^\top \Sigma w$ (variance), subject to $w^\top \mu = \mu_p$ and $\mathbf{1}^\top w = 1$.
   - **Equivalent dual:** Maximize $w^\top \mu - \dfrac{\gamma}{2} w^\top \Sigma w$ (utility).

The set of optimal $(E[R_p], \sigma_p)$ pairs is the **Efficient Frontier**, a hyperbola in the $(\sigma, E[R])$ plane.

## 2.2 Risk Management — Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Sharpe Ratio

- **VaR(α)**: The maximum loss over a horizon such that $\Pr(L \le -\text{VaR}_\alpha) \le 1-\alpha$. For a normally distributed return $R \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ over horizon $h$:
  $$\text{VaR}_\alpha = -(\mu h - z_\alpha \, \sigma \sqrt{h}) \cdot V_0$$
  where $V_0$ is portfolio value and $z_\alpha$ is the standard-normal quantile (e.g., $z_{0.05}=1.645$ for 95% VaR).
- **Sharpe Ratio**: $S = \dfrac{E[R_p] - r_f}{\sigma_p}$. Higher $S$ implies superior risk-adjusted performance.
- **CVaR (Expected Shortfall)**: The *expected* loss given that the loss exceeds VaR. Coherent risk measure, preferred in modern regulation.

## 2.3 Inventory Management — Newsvendor & EOQ

- **Newsvendor (single-period, stochastic demand $D \sim F$)**: Choose order quantity $Q^*$ to maximize expected profit.
- **EOQ (deterministic, multi-period)**: Wilson's formula minimizes total annual cost.
- **(s, S) policy**: Whenever inventory position drops to or below reorder point $s$, order up to $S$. Used when demand is stochastic and replenishment is periodic.

## 2.4 Queuing Systems — Kendall's Notation & Birth–Death Analysis

**Kendall's Notation:** $A/S/c/K/m/Z$ — Arrival / Service / Servers / Capacity / Population / Discipline.
Most KTU questions deal with **M/M/1** (Poisson arrivals, exponential service, 1 server) or **M/M/c** (c servers).

**Birth–Death Process:** State $n$ = number of customers in system. Birth rate $\lambda_n$, death rate $\mu_n$. Steady-state probability $P_n$ exists if $\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete cheat-sheet** you are permitted to carry into your mental exam hall. Every KTU question on Module 4 reduces to one of these.

| # | Domain | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Portfolio | $\sigma_p^2 = w^\top \Sigma w$ | Portfolio variance |
| 2 | Portfolio | $E[R_p] = w^\top \mu$ | Portfolio expected return |
| 3 | Portfolio | $\min_w w^\top \Sigma w$ s.t. $w^\top \mu = \mu_p, \mathbf{1}^\top w = 1$ | Markowitz optimization |
| 4 | Risk | $\text{VaR}_\alpha = V_0 \cdot (z_\alpha \sigma \sqrt{h} - \mu h)$ | Parametric VaR (normal) |
| 5 | Risk | $S = (E[R_p] - r_f)/\sigma_p$ | Sharpe Ratio |
| 6 | Risk | $\text{CVaR}_\alpha = \mu h - \dfrac{\phi(z_\alpha)}{1-\alpha}\sigma\sqrt{h} \cdot V_0$ | Expected Shortfall |
| 7 | Inventory | $Q^* = F^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_u}{c_u+c_o}\right)$ | Newsvendor critical fractile |
| 8 | Inventory | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D K}{h}}$ | Wilson EOQ |
| 9 | Inventory | $R = \mu_D L + z_\alpha \sigma_D \sqrt{L}$ | Reorder point with safety stock |
| 10 | Queue M/M/1 | $P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$ | Steady-state probability |
| 11 | Queue M/M/1 | $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$ | Mean number in system |
| 12 | Queue M/M/1 | $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$ | Mean number in queue |
| 13 | Queue M/M/1 | $W = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$ | Mean time in system (Little's) |
| 14 | Queue M/M/1 | $W_q = \rho/(\mu - \lambda)$ | Mean waiting time |
| 15 | Queue M/M/c | $P_0 = \left[\sum_{k=0}^{c-1}\dfrac{(c\rho)^k}{k!} + \dfrac{(c\rho)^c}{c!}\dfrac{1}{1-\rho}\right]^{-1}$ | Erlang-C idle probability |
| 16 | Queue M/M/c | $L_q = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{(c\rho)^c \, \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2}$ | Mean queue length |
| 17 | Queue | $L = \lambda W$ | **Little's Law** |

> [!NOTE]
> In all queueing formulas, $\lambda$ has units *customers per unit time*, $\mu$ is *services per unit time* per server, and $L, L_q$ are dimensionless (customers). $W, W_q$ have units of *time*.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Portfolio Optimization** is used by sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, robo-advisors, and the RBI's investment cell.
- **Risk Management** is mandated by the **Basel III** accord for all commercial banks computing capital adequacy ratios.
- **Inventory Management** underpins Amazon's fulfillment algorithms, the Indian Public Distribution System (PDS), and Zomato-Swiggy cloud-kitchen stock control.
- **Queuing Theory** is foundational to **call center staffing** (e.g., Airtel BSNL IVR), **hospital ER triage**, **manufacturing assembly-line balancing**, and **5G network packet scheduling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exhaustive**. No algebraic step is skipped, in strict adherence to KTU ESE 14-mark answer expectations.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Markowitz Optimal Weights (Two Risky Assets)

**Problem Setup.** Consider two risky assets with:
- Expected return vector $\mu = \begin{pmatrix} \mu_1 \\ \mu_2 \end{pmatrix}$
- Covariance matrix $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & \rho\sigma_1\sigma_2 \\ \rho\sigma_1\sigma_2 & \sigma_2^2 \end{pmatrix}$
- Weights $w_1, w_2$ with $w_1 + w_2 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Express expected portfolio return.**

$$
E[R_p] = w_1 \mu_1 + w_2 \mu_2 = w_1 \mu_1 + (1-w_1)\mu_2 = \mu_2 + w_1(\mu_1 - \mu_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Express portfolio variance.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $w_2 = 1 - w_1$.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + (1-w_1)^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 (1-w_1) \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the squares.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2w_1\sigma_2^2 + w_1^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 - 2 w_1^2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2
$$

**Step 5 — Group terms in $w_1^2$ and $w_1$.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2)w_1^2 + 2(\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2^2)w_1 + \sigma_2^2
$$

**Step 6 — Minimize w.r.t. $w_1$ by setting $\partial \sigma_p^2 / \partial w_1 = 0$.**

$$
2(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2) w_1 + 2(\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2^2) = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the global minimum-variance weight $w_1^*$.**

$$
w_1^* = \dfrac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho\sigma_1\sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute $w_2^* = 1 - w_1^*$.**

$$
w_2^* = \dfrac{\sigma_1^2 - \rho\sigma_1\sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify diversification benefit.** If $\rho < 1$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$, then $w_1^* = w_2^* = 0.5$ and $\sigma_p = \sigma\sqrt{(1+\rho)/2} < \sigma$ — a **strictly smaller** portfolio variance than either individual asset. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Newsvendor Critical Fractile

**Setup.** Single-period problem. Order $Q$ units at unit cost $c$, sell at price $p$, salvage at $v$ if unsold. Underage cost $c_u = p - c$, overage cost $c_o = c - v$. Random demand $D$ with CDF $F$.

**Step 1 — Write expected profit for a given $Q$.**

$$
\Pi(Q) = E\left[ p \min(Q, D) + v(Q - D)^+ - cQ \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Use the law of total expectation, conditioning on $D$.**

$$
\Pi(Q) = \int_0^Q \left[ p D + v(Q-D) - cQ \right] f(D)\, dD + \int_Q^\infty \left[ p Q - cQ \right] f(D)\, dD
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\Pi(Q)$ w.r.t. $Q$ (Leibniz rule).**

$$
\dfrac{d\Pi}{dQ} = \left[ v(Q - Q) - cQ + cQ \right] f(Q) + \int_Q^\infty (p - c) f(D)\, dD
$$

After simplification, the Leibniz application yields:

$$
\dfrac{d\Pi}{dQ} = -c F(Q) + (p - c)(1 - F(Q)) + v F(Q) - v F(Q)
$$

Collecting terms:

$$
\dfrac{d\Pi}{dQ} = (p - c)(1 - F(Q)) - (c - v) F(Q)
$$

**Step 4 — Set derivative to zero for optimum.**

$$
(p - c)(1 - F(Q^*)) = (c - v) F(Q^*)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $F(Q^*)$.**

$$
F(Q^*) = \dfrac{p - c}{(p - c) + (c - v)} = \dfrac{c_u}{c_u + c_o}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply inverse CDF.**

$$
Q^* = F^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_u}{c_u + c_o}\right)
$$

This is the **critical fractile** solution. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Wilson's EOQ

**Setup.** Annual demand $D$ (units/yr), ordering cost $K$ (₹/order), holding cost $h$ (₹/unit/yr). Order quantity $Q$. Number of orders per year $= D/Q$. Average inventory $= Q/2$.

**Step 1 — Total annual cost function.**

$$
TC(Q) = \underbrace{K \cdot \dfrac{D}{Q}}_{\text{ordering}} + \underbrace{h \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2}}_{\text{holding}}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $Q$ and set to zero.**

$$
\dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{KD}{Q^2} + \dfrac{h}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve.**

$$
Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2KD}{h} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2KD}{h}}
$$

**Step 4 — Second-order check: $d^2TC/dQ^2 = 2KD/Q^3 > 0$ confirms minimum.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Steady-State M/M/1 Probabilities (Birth–Death Balance)

**Setup.** Birth rate $\lambda_n = \lambda$ for $n \ge 0$; death rate $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n \ge 1$.

**Step 1 — Local balance at state $n$ ($n \ge 1$):**

$$
\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n
$$

**Step 2 — For M/M/1, this simplifies to:**

$$
\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange and use the recursion $P_{n+1} = \rho P_n$ by induction.** Base case: $P_1 = \rho P_0$. Assume $P_n = \rho^n P_0$. Then:

$$
\mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n - \lambda P_{n-1} = (\lambda + \mu)\rho^n P_0 - \lambda \rho^{n-1} P_0
$$

**Step 4 — Factor and simplify.**

$$
P_{n+1} = \rho P_n \quad\forall n \ge 0
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize using $\sum_{n=0}^\infty P_n = 1$.**

$$
P_0 \sum_{n=0}^\infty \rho^n = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\rho} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; P_0 = 1-\rho
$$

**Step 6 — Hence $P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$ for all $n \ge 0$.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — M/M/1 Queue

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **archetype question** KTU examiners reuse. Practice it until you can solve it in 90 seconds.

**Problem.** A toll booth serves cars at rate $\mu = 30$/hr. Cars arrive at rate $\lambda = 20$/hr (Poisson). Compute $\rho, P_0, L, L_q, W, W_q, P\{N>5\}$.

**Solution.**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{20}{30} = 0.6667
$$

$$
P_0 = 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \dfrac{0.6667}{0.3333} = 2 \text{ cars}
$$

$$
L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \dfrac{0.4444}{0.3333} = 1.333 \text{ cars}
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{30-20} = 0.1 \text{ hr} = 6 \text{ min}
$$

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{0.6667}{10} = 0.0667 \text{ hr} = 4 \text{ min}
$$

$$
P\{N>5\} = \sum_{n=6}^\infty (1-\rho)\rho^n = \rho^6 = (0.6667)^6 \approx 0.0878
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stochastic Decision-Making Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Environment: Asset Returns, Demand, Arrivals] --> B[Probability Model: Distribution, Stochastic Process]
    B --> C[Performance Metric: Mean, Variance, VaR, L, W]
    C --> D[Optimization Engine: Mean-Variance, Newsvendor, Erlang-C]
    D --> E[Optimal Policy: Weights w, Order Q, Servers c]
    E --> F[Decision Output: Portfolio, Reorder, Staffing]
    F --> G[Feedback Loop: Realized Random Outcomes]
    G --> B
```

> **Figure 4.1.** Closed-loop control system for stochastic decision-making. The environment generates random outcomes that feed back into the probability model, enabling **adaptive re-optimization** — a key concept in reinforcement-learning extensions of this module.

## 4.2 Schematic — Newsvendor Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Realized Demand D] --> Q{Is D greater than or equal to Q*}
    Q -->|Yes| X[Sell Q at full price p: Revenue pq minus cost cQ]
    Q -->|No| Y[Stockout penalty: lose underage cost cu per unit short]
    Q2{D greater than Q*}
    Q2 -->|No| S[Salvage unsold Q minus D units at value v: Overage cost co]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — M/M/1 Queuing System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source: Infinite Population] --> ARR[Poisson Arrivals: rate lambda]
    ARR --> Q1[Queue: FCFS Discipline, Infinite Capacity]
    Q1 --> SVR[Single Server: Exponential Service rate mu]
    SVR --> SNK[Sink after Service]
    SVR -.-> MET[Performance Outputs: rho, L, Lq, W, Wq, Pn]
    Q1 -.-> MET
    ARR -.-> MET
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Portfolio Optimization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input: Asset Returns, Covariance Matrix, Risk Aversion] --> EST[Step 1: Estimate Mu and Sigma from Historical Data]
    EST --> FRT[Step 2: Compute Efficient Frontier by Sweeping Target Return]
    FRT --> OPT[Step 3: Solve Quadratic Program for Optimal Weights w]
    OPT --> TANG[Step 4: Add Risk-Free Asset to find Tangency Portfolio]
    TANG --> CAP[Step 5: Derive Capital Market Line CML]
    CAP --> OUT[Output: Optimal Allocation, Sharpe Ratio, VaR]
```

## 4.5 Architecture — Reorder Point System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MON[Continuous Inventory Monitoring] --> CHK{Current Position less than or equal to ROP}
    CHK -->|No| WAIT[Wait and Continue Monitoring]
    CHK -->|Yes| ORD[Place Order of Q units]
    ORD --> LD[Lead Time L: Stochastic]
    LD --> RCV[Receive Order: Inventory Replenished]
    RCV --> MON
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct-recall questions modeled on KTU university exam Part A. Each carries **3 marks** and demands a 4–6 line answer.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECAT867]**
Define the **Sharpe Ratio** of a portfolio. A portfolio has expected return 12% per annum, standard deviation 18%, and the risk-free rate is 6%. Compute the Sharpe Ratio and interpret the result.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Sharpe Ratio measures excess return per unit of total risk:

$$
S = \dfrac{E[R_p] - r_f}{\sigma_p} = \dfrac{0.12 - 0.06}{0.18} = 0.333
$$

**Interpretation:** The portfolio delivers 0.333 units of excess return for every unit of risk; this is acceptable for an aggressive equity portfolio but mediocre versus hedge-fund benchmarks (which typically achieve $S \ge 0.5$).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECAT867]**
State **Kendall's Notation** for a queuing system. Explain the meaning of each symbol in **M/M/1/K**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Kendall's notation is $A/S/c/K/m/Z$ where:
- $A$ = Arrival process (M = Markovian/Poisson)
- $S$ = Service-time distribution (M = exponential)
- $c$ = Number of parallel servers
- $K$ = System capacity
- $m$ = Source population size
- $Z$ = Queue discipline

For **M/M/1/K**: Poisson arrivals, exponential service times, **1** server, **K**-capacity system. If $K=\infty$, the system is the standard M/M/1.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B carries a **Module-level internal choice**: you may answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both are designed for the full 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Portfolio + Risk Management

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECAT867, Module 4]**

A portfolio manager considers two risky assets A and B with the following characteristics:

| Asset | $E[R_i]$ | $\sigma_i$ | $\rho_{AB}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 15% | 20% | 0.3 |
| B | 10% | 15% | — |

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Formulate and solve** the Markowitz minimum-variance portfolio. State the weights, the portfolio expected return, and the portfolio standard deviation.

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
The manager also holds a risk-free asset with $r_f = 5\%$. **Derive the Capital Market Line (CML)** and compute the Sharpe Ratio of the tangency portfolio. If the manager wants a target expected return of 12%, find the optimal allocation between the tangency portfolio and the risk-free asset.

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Compute covariance.**

$$
\sigma_{AB} = \rho_{AB}\sigma_A \sigma_B = 0.3 \times 0.20 \times 0.15 = 0.009
$$

$$
\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 0.04 & 0.009 \\ 0.009 & 0.0225 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply minimum-variance weight formula (from Section 3.1).**

$$
w_A^* = \dfrac{\sigma_B^2 - \sigma_{AB}}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2\sigma_{AB}} = \dfrac{0.0225 - 0.009}{0.04 + 0.0225 - 0.018} = \dfrac{0.0135}{0.0445} = 0.3034
$$

$$
w_B^* = 1 - 0.3034 = 0.6966
$$

**Step 3 — Expected return.**

$$
E[R_p] = 0.3034 \times 0.15 + 0.6966 \times 0.10 = 0.0455 + 0.0697 = 0.1152 = 11.52\%
$$

**Step 4 — Portfolio variance.**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (0.3034)^2(0.04) + (0.6966)^2(0.0225) + 2(0.3034)(0.6966)(0.009)
$$

$$
= 0.003683 + 0.010915 + 0.003804 = 0.018402
$$

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.018402} = 0.1357 = 13.57\%
$$

**Conclusion (Part a):** The minimum-variance portfolio has weights **30.34% in A and 69.66% in B**, with expected return **11.52%** and standard deviation **13.57%** — strictly less risky than either individual asset.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Maximum Sharpe Ratio (tangency portfolio) requires maximizing $(w^\top \mu - r_f)/\sqrt{w^\top \Sigma w}$.**

The optimal tangency weights are:

$$
w_T = \dfrac{\Sigma^{-1}(\mu - r_f \mathbf{1})}{\mathbf{1}^\top \Sigma^{-1}(\mu - r_f \mathbf{1})}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Sigma^{-1}$.**

$$
\det(\Sigma) = (0.04)(0.0225) - (0.009)^2 = 0.0009 - 0.000081 = 0.000819
$$

$$
\Sigma^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{0.000819}\begin{pmatrix} 0.0225 & -0.009 \\ -0.009 & 0.04 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 27.47 & -10.99 \\ -10.99 & 48.84 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mu - r_f \mathbf{1} = (0.10, 0.05)^\top$.**

$$
\Sigma^{-1}(\mu - r_f\mathbf{1}) = \begin{pmatrix} 27.47 \times 0.10 - 10.99 \times 0.05 \\ -10.99 \times 0.10 + 48.84 \times 0.05 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.197 \\ 1.343 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize.**

$$
\mathbf{1}^\top \Sigma^{-1}(\mu - r_f\mathbf{1}) = 2.197 + 1.343 = 3.540
$$

$$
w_T = (2.197/3.540, 1.343/3.540)^\top = (0.6206, 0.3794)^\top
$$

**Step 5 — Tangency expected return and standard deviation.**

$$
E[R_T] = 0.6206 \times 0.15 + 0.3794 \times 0.10 = 0.0931 + 0.0379 = 0.1310 = 13.10\%
$$

$$
\sigma_T^2 = (0.6206)^2(0.04) + (0.3794)^2(0.0225) + 2(0.6206)(0.3794)(0.009) = 0.01541 + 0.00324 + 0.00424 = 0.02289
$$

$$
\sigma_T = \sqrt{0.02289} = 0.1513 = 15.13\%
$$

**Step 6 — Sharpe Ratio of Tangency.**

$$
S_T = \dfrac{0.1310 - 0.05}{0.1513} = \dfrac{0.0810}{0.1513} = 0.5355
$$

**Step 7 — CML equation:**

$$
E[R_C] = r_f + S_T \sigma_C = 0.05 + 0.5355 \sigma_C
$$

**Step 8 — For target $E[R_C] = 12\%$, solve for $\sigma_C$ and weights.**

$$
\sigma_C = \dfrac{0.12 - 0.05}{0.5355} = 0.1307 = 13.07\%
$$

Weight in tangency portfolio: $y = (E[R_C] - r_f)/(E[R_T] - r_f) = (0.12 - 0.05)/(0.1310 - 0.05) = 0.07/0.0810 = 0.8642$.

Weight in risk-free asset: $1 - y = 0.1358$.

**Final allocation:** **86.42% in tangency portfolio (62.06% A + 37.94% B → 53.7% A, 32.8% B)** and **13.58% in risk-free asset**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Inventory + Queuing

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECAT867, Module 4]**

A bakery sells fresh cakes at ₹400 each. Cakes are made at a cost of ₹200 each, and unsold cakes at day's end are salvaged at ₹50 each (donated). Daily demand follows a **normal distribution** with mean 50 cakes and standard deviation 10 cakes.

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
Using the **Newsvendor Model**, compute the optimal daily production quantity $Q^*$. Also compute the **expected profit** if this quantity is produced.

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
Customers arrive at the bakery counter at a Poisson rate of 12 per hour. Service time is exponential with mean 6 minutes. Model this as an **M/M/1** queue. Compute the **utilization $\rho$, mean number in system $L$, mean waiting time $W_q$, and the probability that an arriving customer finds the system empty.**

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Identify costs.**
- Price $p = 400$, cost $c = 200$, salvage $v = 50$.
- Underage cost $c_u = p - c = 200$.
- Overage cost $c_o = c - v = 150$.

**Step 2 — Critical fractile.**

$$
F(Q^*) = \dfrac{c_u}{c_u + c_o} = \dfrac{200}{200+150} = \dfrac{200}{350} = 0.5714
$$

**Step 3 — Find $Q^*$ from standard normal.** $z = \Phi^{-1}(0.5714) = 0.1800$.

$$
Q^* = \mu_D + z \sigma_D = 50 + 0.18 \times 10 = 51.8 \approx 52 \text{ cakes}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected profit calculation.**

Expected sales $= E[\min(D, Q)] = \mu_D - \sigma_D L(z)$ where $L(z) = \phi(z) - z(1-\Phi(z))$ is the standard normal loss function.

For $z = 0.18$: $\phi(0.18) = 0.3925$, $1-\Phi(0.18) = 0.4286$, $L(0.18) = 0.3925 - 0.18 \times 0.4286 = 0.3153$.

Expected sales $= 50 - 10 \times 0.3153 = 46.85$ cakes.

$$
\Pi = 400(46.85) + 50(52-46.85) - 200(52) = 18740 + 257.5 - 10400 = 8597.5
$$

**Expected Profit ≈ ₹8,597.50 per day.**

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Convert to consistent units (per hour).**
- $\lambda = 12$/hr
- Mean service time $= 6$ min $= 0.1$ hr, so $\mu = 1/0.1 = 10$/hr.

**Step 2 — Utilization.**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{12}{10} = 1.2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL EXAMINER'S PITFALL** — When $\rho \ge 1$, the system is **unstable**: the queue grows unboundedly and $L, W_q$ become **infinite**. A student who blindly substitutes into $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$ here will obtain a *negative number* and lose 4 marks for missing the fundamental stability condition. **Always check $\rho < 1$ first.**

**Step 3 — Verdict.** Since $\rho = 1.2 > 1$, the **single-server system is overloaded**. We must add a server. Re-model as **M/M/2**:

**M/M/2 Resolved:**

$$
\rho_{2\text{-server}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\mu} = \dfrac{12}{20} = 0.6 \quad (< 1, \text{ stable})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $P_0$ for M/M/2 using Erlang-C formula.**

$$
a = \lambda/\mu = 1.2, \quad c = 2
$$

$$
P_0 = \left[ \dfrac{a^0}{0!} + \dfrac{a^1}{1!} + \dfrac{a^2}{2!}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + 1.2 + \dfrac{1.44}{2 \times 0.4} \right]^{-1} = [1 + 1.2 + 1.8]^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 5 — Mean queue length $L_q$.**

$$
L_q = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{a^c \, \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2} = 0.25 \cdot \dfrac{(1.2)^2 (0.6)}{2 \times 0.16} = 0.25 \cdot \dfrac{0.864}{0.32} = 0.25 \times 2.7 = 0.675
$$

**Step 6 — $L = L_q + \rho \cdot c = 0.675 + 0.6 \times 2 = 1.875$ customers.**

**Step 7 — Mean waiting time via Little's Law.**

$$
W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.675}{12} = 0.0563 \text{ hr} = 3.375 \text{ min}
$$

$$
W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 0.0563 + 0.1 = 0.1563 \text{ hr} = 9.375 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8 — Probability system is empty.**

$$
P_0 = 0.25 = 25\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Most students mix up $\lambda = 12$/hr and $\mu = 10$/hr with $\mu = 6$/hr and $\mu = 10$/min. Always convert service time to **the same time unit as the arrival rate**.
> 2. **Stability check** — Always report whether $\rho < 1$. This is worth 2 marks on its own.
> 3. **Stating Little's Law** — You must explicitly write $L = \lambda W$ before using it, or you will lose 1 mark for "unstated assumption."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- 🔹 **Markowitz Portfolio** — weights sum to 1; minimize $w^\top \Sigma w$ subject to target return. **Diversification** reduces variance iff $\rho < 1$.
- 🔹 **Sharpe Ratio** — single number summarizing risk-adjusted performance. Tangency portfolio maximizes it.
- 🔹 **VaR vs CVaR** — VaR is a quantile, CVaR is the conditional mean beyond it. CVaR is *coherent*; VaR is not.
- 🔹 **Newsvendor $Q^*$** — $F(Q^*) = c_u/(c_u + c_o)$. The **critical fractile** is the newsvendor's defining principle.
- 🔹 **EOQ** — Wilson's formula $Q^* = \sqrt{2KD/h}$. Assumes **deterministic** demand and **instantaneous** replenishment.
- 🔹 **Reorder Point with Safety Stock** — $R = \mu_D L + z_\alpha \sigma_D \sqrt{L}$. The $\sqrt{L}$ arises from independent period-by-period demand.
- 🔹 **Kendall's Notation** — $A/S/c$. The most-tested system is **M/M/1** ($\rho < 1$ required).
- 🔹 **Little's Law** — $L = \lambda W$. Universally valid for any stable queue in steady state.
- 🔹 **M/M/1 formulas** — $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $W = 1/(\mu-\lambda)$, $W_q = \rho/(\mu-\lambda)$.
- 🔹 **Erlang-C** — used for **M/M/c** multi-server systems; memorize $P_0$ and $L_q$ expressions.
- 🔹 **Stability rule** — always verify $\rho < 1$ **before** applying any M/M/1 formula; otherwise report the system is **unstable**.
- 🔹 **Unit consistency** — keep $\lambda, \mu$ in the same time unit (per hour or per minute, never mixed).
- 🔹 **KTU marker preferences** — show the **objective function**, list **constraints**, then solve. Three explicit steps earn full marks.
- 🔹 **Real-world anchors** — bankers use VaR; manufacturers use EOQ/(s,S); hospitals use Erlang-C. Linking math to the **domain** fetches 1–2 "explanation" marks.
